title.zh: 卷三十下 郎顗襄楷列傳
title.en: Volume 30b: Biographies of Lang Yi; Xiang Kai
title.raw: 卷三十下 郎顗襄楷列傳 Volume 30b: Biographies of Lang Yi; Xiang Kai

sentences.zh: 郎顗字雅光，北海安丘人也。

translations.literal: Lang Yi, courtesy name Yaguang, was a native of Anqiu in Beihai.
translations.idiomatic: Lang Yi, courtesy Yaguang, came from Anqiu in Beihai commandery.

sentences.zh: 父宗，字仲綏，學《京氏易》，善風角、星算、六日七分，能望氣占候吉凶，常賣卜自奉。

translations.literal: His father Zong, courtesy name Zhongsui, studied the Jingshi interpretation of the Changes, was skilled in wind-angle divination, star reckoning, and the six-day seven-part method, could observe vapors and prognosticate good and ill, and regularly sold divinations to support himself.
translations.idiomatic: His father Lang Zong (courtesy Zhongsui) studied the Jing interpretation of the Book of Changes, mastered wind-angle astrology, star calculation, and the six-day-seven-part calendar, read qi for omens, and made his living as a fortuneteller.

sentences.zh: 安帝徵之，對策為諸儒表，後拜吳令。

translations.literal: Emperor An summoned him; he answered policy questions and stood first among the Ru, afterward being appointed magistrate of Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor An summoned him; he topped the policy examination among classicists and was later made magistrate of Wu.

sentences.zh: 時卒有暴風，宗占知京師當有大火，記識時日，遣人參候，果如其言。

translations.literal: At that time there suddenly came a violent gale; Zong divined that the capital would have a great fire, noted the date and time, sent men to verify—and it was indeed as he said.
translations.idiomatic: When a sudden gale struck, Lang Zong predicted a major fire in the capital, recorded the expected moment, and sent observers to confirm—events matched his forecast.

sentences.zh: 諸公聞而表上，以博士徵之。

translations.literal: The high officials heard of it and memorialized upward; he was summoned as an Erudite.
translations.idiomatic: The dignitaries reported his feat to the throne and secured an Erudite’s summons for him.

sentences.zh: 宗恥以占驗見知，聞徵書到，夜縣印綬於縣廷而遁去，遂終身不仕。

translations.literal: Zong was ashamed to be known through divinatory verification; when he heard the summons had arrived, by night he hung his seal-ribbon in the county court and fled, and to the end of his life did not take office.
translations.idiomatic: Lang Zong disdained fame from fortune-telling; when the appointment arrived he hung his official seal in the yamen overnight and vanished, never serving again.

sentences.zh: 顗少傳父業，兼明經典，隱居海畔，延致學徒常數百人。

translations.literal: Yi in his youth transmitted his father’s craft, while also understanding the classics; he dwelt in seclusion by the sea and constantly had several hundred pupils whom he brought in.
translations.idiomatic: The younger Lang Yi inherited his father’s arts while mastering the classics; he lived reclusively on the coast and drew hundreds of students.

sentences.zh: 晝研精義，夜占象度，勤心銳思，朝夕無倦。

translations.literal: By day he investigated refined meanings; by night he observed configurations and measures—his mind was diligent, his thought keen, morning and evening without weariness.
translations.idiomatic: He plumbed doctrine by day and scanned the skies by night, tireless from dawn to dusk.

sentences.zh: 州郡辟召，舉有道、方正，不就。

translations.literal: Provinces and commanderies summoned him and nominated him as possessed of the Way and as forthright; he did not go.
translations.idiomatic: He turned down every provincial summons and recommendation for “possessed of the Way” or “forthright.”

sentences.zh: 順帝時，災異屢見，陽嘉二年正月，公正徵，顗乃詣闕拜章曰：

translations.literal: During Emperor Shun’s time, calamities and prodigies appeared repeatedly; in the first month of Yangjia 2, when the equitable summons went out, Yi then went to the palace and submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Shun, omens multiplied; in the first month of Yangjia 2, when the court called for candidacies, Lang Yi presented himself at the gates and offered this memorial:

sentences.zh: 臣聞天垂妖象，地見災符，所以譴告人主，責躬修德，使正機平衡，流化興政也。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that Heaven suspends prodigious images and Earth reveals calamitous tokens in order to reprove the ruler, make him reproach himself and cultivate virtue, set straight the pivot and balance the scales, and extend transformation and raise good government.
translations.idiomatic: I have learned that Heaven hangs strange signs and Earth shows warning marks to admonish the sovereign— urging self-scrutiny, moral cultivation, true balance in rule, and reform that spreads virtue.

sentences.zh: 《易內傳》曰：『凡災異所生，各以其政。

translations.literal: The Inner Commentary on the Changes states: ‘Whatever calamities and prodigies arise, each matches its government.
translations.idiomatic: The Inner Commentary on the Book of Changes says: “Every disaster springs from the kind of governance that produced it.”

sentences.zh: 變之則除，消之亦除。

translations.literal: Change them and they are removed; dispel them and they are also removed.
translations.idiomatic: Reform the policies and the omens lift; eliminate the faults and they lift as well.”

sentences.zh: 』伏惟陛下躬日吳之聽，溫三省之勤，思過念咎，務消祇悔。

translations.literal: ’ Bowing in thought, Your Majesty listens with the diligence of one who attends until the sun declines, warms the thrice-daily self-examination, reflects on faults and thinks of blame, and strives to dispel root regret.
translations.idiomatic: “I trust Your Majesty hears petitions with the zeal of one who sits past midday, renews the thrice-daily self-scrutiny, weighs missteps, and works to clear enduring regret.”

sentences.zh: 方今時俗奢佚，淺恩薄義。

translations.literal: At present custom is extravagant and dissolute; kindness is shallow and duty thin.
translations.idiomatic: Today’s habits run to extravagance and ease; compassion is shallow and righteousness slight.

sentences.zh: 夫救奢必於儉約，拯薄無若敦厚，安上理人，莫善於禮。

translations.literal: To rescue extravagance nothing surpasses frugal restraint; to redeem thinness nothing equals fostering generous solidity; to settle superiors and order the people nothing is better than ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Extravagance must be cured by austerity; moral thinness by earnest generosity; governing rulers and people alike begins with ritual.

sentences.zh: 修禮遵約，蓋惟上興，革文變薄，事不在下。

translations.literal: Cultivating ritual and observing restraint rises from above; altering ornament and changing thinness is not a task for those below.
translations.idiomatic: Ritual discipline must start with the throne; trimming display and shallow ways cannot be left to commoners alone.

sentences.zh: 故《周南》之德，《關雎》政本。

translations.literal: Therefore the virtue of the ‘Zhou South,’ ‘Guanju’ is the root of government.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the moral tone of the “Zhou South,” where “Guanju” stands as the foundation of policy.

sentences.zh: 本立道生，風行草從，澄其源者流清，混其本者末濁。

translations.literal: When the root is set, the Way is born; when the wind blows, the grass bends—whoever clarifies the source has clear currents; whoever muddies the root has turbid ends.
translations.idiomatic: Establish the root and the Way grows; as wind bends grass, so pure springs flow from a clean headwaters and foul streams from a tainted source.

sentences.zh: 天地之道，其猶鼓籥，以虛為德，自近及遠者也。

translations.literal: The Way of Heaven and Earth is like the drum and pipe: it takes emptiness as its virtue and proceeds from the near to the far.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven and Earth work like a bellows and flute—power lies in modest emptiness, and influence spreads outward from the center.

sentences.zh: 伏見往年以來，園陵數災，炎光熾猛，驚動神靈。

translations.literal: Your subject has seen that in recent years the imperial parks and tombs have repeatedly suffered disaster; flames burned fiercely and shook the spirits.
translations.idiomatic: I note repeated fires at the mausolea—blazes fierce enough to disturb the imperial dead.

sentences.zh: 《易天人應》曰：『君子不思遵利，茲謂無澤，厥災孽火燒其宮。

translations.literal: The Correlation of Heaven and Man in the Changes states: ‘If the gentleman does not reflect on following profit, this is called lacking grace; then the calamitous fire burns his palace.
translations.idiomatic: The Changes’ “Heaven and Man Correlation” warns: “When rulers chase profit without reflection, grace dries up—and destructive fire consumes the palace.”

sentences.zh: 』又曰：『君高臺府，犯陰侵陽，厥災火。

translations.literal: ’ It also states: ‘If the lord raises high terraces and treasuries, encroaching yin upon yang, the calamity is fire.
translations.idiomatic: It adds: “Lofty towers and lavish treasuries violate yin–yang balance—fire follows.”

sentences.zh: 』又曰：『上不儉，下不節，炎火並作燒君室。

translations.literal: ’ It also states: ‘If those above are not frugal and those below are not restrained, raging fires together burn the ruler’s chambers.
translations.idiomatic: And: “When neither sovereign nor subjects practice restraint, fires rage through the ruler’s halls.”

sentences.zh: 』自頃繕理西蒼，修復太學，宮殿官府，多所構飾。

translations.literal: ’ Recently you have repaired the Western Granary, restored the Imperial Academy, and ornamented many palace halls and government offices.
translations.idiomatic: Lately the Western Granary has been rebuilt, the Imperial University restored, and countless offices and halls refurbished.

sentences.zh: 昔盤庚遷殷，去奢即儉，夏後卑室，盡力致美。

translations.literal: Formerly Pan Geng moved to Yin, leaving extravagance for thrift; the lord of Xia kept humble chambers yet strove for perfect beauty.
translations.idiomatic: Pan Geng left excess for simplicity when he moved the capital; the Xia founders kept modest rooms yet pursued true refinement.

sentences.zh: 又魯人為長府，閔子騫曰：『仍舊貫，何必改作。

translations.literal: Further, when the people of Lu enlarged the Long Treasury, Min Ziqian said: ‘Follow the old measure—why must we rebuild?
translations.idiomatic: When Lu expanded its treasury, Min Ziqian objected: “Keep the old scale—why rebuild?”

sentences.zh: 』臣愚以為諸所繕修，事可省減，稟恤貧人，賑贍孤寡，此天之意也，人之慶也，仁之本也，儉之要也。

translations.literal: ’ Your subject foolishly thinks that every project now under repair could be cut back; relieve and pity the poor, aid widows and orphans—this is Heaven’s intent, humanity’s blessing, the root of benevolence, and the essence of thrift.
translations.idiomatic: I urge cutting every repair still possible, easing taxes on the destitute, feeding orphans and widows—that answers Heaven, brings joy to men, anchors humanity, and embodies austerity.

sentences.zh: 焉有應天養人，為仁為儉，而不降福者哉？

translations.literal: How could there be one who answers Heaven and nurtures the people, thereby practicing benevolence and thrift, yet does not receive blessing?
translations.idiomatic: Who could heed Heaven, care for the people, embrace kindness and restraint, and still lack Heaven’s favor?

sentences.zh: 土者地祇，陰性澄靜，宜以施化之時，敬而勿擾。

translations.literal: Earth is the god of soil; its yin nature is clear and still—it ought in the season of spreading transformation to be revered and not disturbed.
translations.idiomatic: Earth is the soil deity—yin, calm, and quiet; in seasons meant for nurturing growth it should be honored, not tormented.

sentences.zh: 竊見正月以來，陰暗連日。

translations.literal: Privately your subject has seen that since the first month gloom has lasted many days in succession.
translations.idiomatic: Since New Year’s month darkness has stretched day after day.

sentences.zh: 《易內傳》曰：『久陰不雨，亂氣也，《蒙》之《比》也。

translations.literal: The Inner Commentary on the Changes states: ‘Long gloom without rain is chaotic qi—the hexagram Meng becoming Bi.
translations.idiomatic: The Inner Commentary says: “Persistent overcast without rain marks confused qi—Meng turning into Bi.”

sentences.zh: 蒙者，君臣上下相冒亂也。

translations.literal: Meng means ruler and ministers, high and low, masking one another in disorder.
translations.idiomatic: Meng pictures superiors and subordinates obscuring each other in chaos.

sentences.zh: 』又曰：『欲德不用，厥異常陰。

translations.literal: ’ It also states: ‘When virtue is desired yet unused, the anomaly is constant gloom.
translations.idiomatic: It adds: “Wanted virtue left unused brings endless murk.”

sentences.zh: 』夫賢者化之本，雲者雨之具也。

translations.literal: ’ Worthy men are the root of transformation; clouds are the vessel of rain.
translations.idiomatic: The worthy are transformation’s root; clouds hold the rain.

sentences.zh: 得賢而不用，猶久陰而不雨也。

translations.literal: To obtain worthies yet not employ them is like long gloom without rain.
translations.idiomatic: Ignoring talent while claiming to seek it matches endless cloud with no downpour.

sentences.zh: 又頃前數日，寒過其節，冰既解釋，還復凝合。

translations.literal: Further, in recent days cold exceeded its proper measure; ice already melting again froze solid.
translations.idiomatic: Recently cold overshot its season—ice thawed, then locked tight again.

sentences.zh: 夫寒往則暑來，暑往則寒來，此言日月相推，寒暑相避，以成物也。

translations.literal: When cold departs, heat arrives; when heat departs, cold arrives—this speaks of sun and moon pushing one another, cold and heat yielding in turn, thereby completing things.
translations.idiomatic: Cold yields to heat and heat to cold—sun and moon alternate, seasons hand off, and creatures mature.

sentences.zh: 今立春之後，火卦用事，當溫而寒，違反時節，由功賞不至，而刑罰必加也。

translations.literal: Now after the Beginning of Spring the trigram of Fire administers affairs—it ought to be warm yet it is cold, violating the seasonal nodes—because merit rewards do not reach the people while punishments are relentlessly applied.
translations.idiomatic: After spring begins the fire phase should rule—yet chill persists. The fault lies in withholding rewards while inflicting harsh penalties.

sentences.zh: 宜須立秋，順氣行罰。

translations.literal: One ought to wait until the Beginning of Autumn, conforming to qi in applying punishments.
translations.idiomatic: Defer executions until autumn so punishments align with seasonal qi.

sentences.zh: 臣伏案《飛侯》，參察眾政，以為立夏之後，當有震裂湧水之害。

translations.literal: Your subject has bent over the Flying Marquis divination and surveyed many policies; he believes that after the Beginning of Summer there ought to be harm from cracking earth and surging waters.
translations.idiomatic: Reviewing the Flying Marquis charts and current policy, I forecast earthquakes and floods after summer begins.

sentences.zh: 又比熒惑失度，盈縮往來，涉歷輿鬼，環繞軒轅。

translations.literal: Moreover recently the Sparkling Deluder has missed its measure, waxing and waning back and forth, crossing Ghost Cart and circling the Chariot.
translations.idiomatic: Mars wanders off course, swelling and shrinking as it crosses Ghost constellation and loops the imperial asterism.

sentences.zh: 火精南方，夏之政也。

translations.literal: The essence of fire lies south—it is the government of summer.
translations.idiomatic: Fire’s essence rules the south—it governs summer’s administration.

sentences.zh: 政有失禮，不從夏令，則熒惑失行。

translations.literal: If government loses propriety and does not follow summer commands, then the Sparkling Deluder loses its course.
translations.idiomatic: When summer governance goes awry, Mars strays from its path.

sentences.zh: 正月三日至乎九日，三公卦也。

translations.literal: The third through ninth days of the first month are the trigram of the Three Excellencies.
translations.idiomatic: The third through ninth days of the first month correspond to the Three Excellencies’ hexagram.

sentences.zh: 三公上應臺階，下同元首。

translations.literal: The Three Excellencies correspond above to the Terraces and below join with the sovereign’s head.
translations.idiomatic: The three dukes mirror the celestial Terraces and bind the ruler’s person.

sentences.zh: 政失其道，則寒陰反節。

translations.literal: If government misses the Way, then cold yin violates its proper measure.
translations.idiomatic: Misrule lets yin cold overturn seasonal order.

sentences.zh: 『節彼南山』，詠自《周詩》；

translations.literal: ‘High as those southern hills’—the ode comes from the Zhou poems;
translations.idiomatic: “Lofty as those southern hills”—that ode belongs to the Zhou hymns;

sentences.zh: 『股肱良哉』，著於《虞典》。

translations.literal: ‘Our limbs are good’—it is recorded in the Canon of Yu.
translations.idiomatic: “Our limbs are worthy”—the Canon of Shun praises such ministers.

sentences.zh: 而今之在位，競托高虛，納累鐘之奉，忘天下之憂，棲遲偃仰，寢疾自逸，被策文，得賜錢，即復起矣。

translations.literal: Yet those now in office vie to pose lofty and empty, pocket stipends equal to stacked bells, forget the realm’s worries, recline at ease feigning illness, and when edicts grant cash they rise at once.
translations.idiomatic: Today’s officials strike grand poses, draw fat salaries, ignore the empire’s troubles, lounge about pleading sickness, yet leap up when bonus payments arrive.

sentences.zh: 何疾之易而愈之速』以此消伏災眚，興致升平，其可得乎？

translations.literal: How can illness be so easy and recovery so swift?’ By this to dispel disasters and bring rising peace—can it be achieved?
translations.idiomatic: Can ailments vanish that conveniently if sincerity were real? With such conduct, can omens be banished and peace restored?

sentences.zh: 今選舉牧守，委任三府。

translations.literal: Now selection of shepherds and guardians is entrusted to the Three Bureaus.
translations.idiomatic: Choosing governors rests with the three high offices.

sentences.zh: 長吏不良，既咎州郡，州郡有失，豈得不歸責舉者？

translations.literal: If senior clerks are bad, blame falls on province and commandery; when province and commandery err, can responsibility fail to return to those who recommended them?
translations.idiomatic: Bad magistrates indict their superiors; faulty provinces indict their nominators—should not the buck stop at the recommenders?

sentences.zh: 而陛下崇之彌優，自下慢事愈甚，所謂大網疏，小網數。

translations.literal: Yet Your Majesty treats them ever more generously while those below slight their duties all the more—this is what is called a wide net above and a tight net below.
translations.idiomatic: The throne heaps favor on them while underlings grow lazier—wide meshes above, tight meshes below.

sentences.zh: 三公非臣之仇，臣非狂夫之作，所以發憤忘食，懇懇不已者，誠念朝廷欲致興平，非不能面譽也。

translations.literal: The Three Excellencies are not your subject’s enemies, nor is your subject the work of a madman; the reason he forgets food in wrath and cannot cease earnest pleading is that he truly remembers how the court desires rising peace—not that he cannot praise to your face.
translations.idiomatic: The three dukes are not my foes and I am no fanatic; I speak because the court hungers for stable rule, not because flattery is impossible.

sentences.zh: 臣生長草野，不曉禁忌，披露肝膽，書不擇言。

translations.literal: Your subject grew among weeds and wilds and does not understand taboos; he exposes liver and gall and writes without choosing words.
translations.idiomatic: Raised in the backcountry, ignorant of court taboos, I lay my heart bare without polishing phrases.

sentences.zh: 伏锧鼎鑊，死不敢恨。

translations.literal: He submits to the chopping block and cauldron and dies without resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Let me face axe or cauldron—I die without complaint.

sentences.zh: 謹詣闕奉章，伏待重誅。

translations.literal: Respectfully he has come to the palace to present his memorial and awaits repeated execution.
translations.idiomatic: I proffer this memorial at the gate and await whatever sentence you decree.

sentences.zh: 書奏，帝復使對尚書。

translations.literal: When the document was presented, the emperor again had him answer before the Masters of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: After it was read, the emperor ordered him to respond before the Masters of Writing.

sentences.zh: 顗對曰：

translations.literal: Yi replied:
translations.idiomatic: Lang Yi answered:

sentences.zh: 臣聞明王聖主好聞其過，忠臣孝子言無隱情。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that enlightened kings and sage lords love to hear their faults, and loyal ministers and filial sons speak without hiding feelings.
translations.idiomatic: Wise rulers welcome criticism; loyal servants hide nothing.

sentences.zh: 臣備生人倫視聽之類，而稟性愚愨，不識忌諱，故出死亡命，懇懇重言。

translations.literal: Your subject stands among the classes of men endowed with human relations and sight and hearing, yet his nature is foolishly honest and he does not know what is forbidden; therefore he ventures death and speaks with earnest repetition.
translations.idiomatic: I am but a common mortal, blunt and unaware of court prohibitions, so I risk death to repeat these truths.

sentences.zh: 誠欲陛下修乾坤之德，開日月之明，披圖籍，案經典，覽帝王之務，識先後之政。

translations.literal: Truly he desires Your Majesty to cultivate the virtue of Qian and Kun, open the brightness of sun and moon, spread out charts and records, consult the classics, survey the tasks of emperors, and know the government of former and later ages.
translations.idiomatic: I pray you enlarge the virtues of Heaven and Earth, shine like sun and moon, study archives and canons, weigh ancient and modern rule.

sentences.zh: 如有闕遺，退而自改。

translations.literal: If there are gaps or oversights, withdraw and correct them yourself.
translations.idiomatic: Where policy falters, amend it yourself.

sentences.zh: 本文、武之業，擬堯、舜之道，攘災延慶，號令天下。

translations.literal: Root yourself in the enterprises of Wen and Wu, model yourself on the Way of Yao and Shun, push aside calamity and prolong blessing, and command the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Ground yourself in Wen and Wu’s legacy, rise toward Yao and Shun’s standard, banish disaster, extend grace, and command the world.

sentences.zh: 此誠臣顗區區之願，夙夜夢寤，盡心所計。

translations.literal: This is truly your subject Yi’s humble wish; night and day, waking or sleeping, he exhausts what his mind can plan.
translations.idiomatic: This humble wish consumes me waking or sleeping.

sentences.zh: 謹條序前章，暢其旨趣，條便宜七事，具如狀對：

translations.literal: Respectfully he arrays and orders the prior chapter, clarifies its intent, and lists seven convenient measures in detail as answered in the memorial:
translations.idiomatic: I therefore expand my earlier argument and submit seven practical measures as follows:

sentences.zh: 一事：陵園至重，聖神攸馮，而災火炎赫，迫近寢殿，魂而有靈，猶將驚動。

translations.literal: First matter: the mausoleum parks are supremely weighty—sage spirits rely on them—yet calamity fire blazed fiercely, pressing near the sleeping hall; if souls have awareness, they would still be shaken.
translations.idiomatic: First: imperial tombs are sacred, yet infernos licked the bedchambers—those spirits would tremble if conscious.

sentences.zh: 尋宮殿宮府，近始永平，歲時未積，便更修造。

translations.literal: Search out the palace halls and offices—they began only in the Yongping era; the years are not yet piled up, yet already they are rebuilt again.
translations.idiomatic: Palaces date barely to Emperor Ming’s Yongping reign—few decades yet endless rebuilding.

sentences.zh: 又西苑之設，禽畜是處，離房別觀，本不常居，而皆條精土木，營建無已，消功單賄，巨億為計。

translations.literal: Further, the Western Park establishment keeps birds and beasts everywhere; detached lodges and separate vistas were never meant for constant residence, yet every item is crafted of finest timber and earthworks never cease—merit wasted, bribes exhausted, sums reckoned in hundreds of millions.
translations.idiomatic: The Western Park pens game and fowl in villas never meant for permanent living, yet carpentry never stops—labor and treasure pour out by the hundred million.

sentences.zh: 《易內傳》曰：『人君奢侈，多飾宮室，其時旱，其災火。

translations.literal: The Inner Commentary on the Changes states: ‘When the lord of men is extravagant and over-decorates palace chambers, of its season there is drought; its calamity is fire.
translations.idiomatic: The Inner Commentary says: “Extravagant rulers who overbuild invite drought and fire.”

sentences.zh: 』是故魯僖遭旱，修政自敕，下鐘鼓之縣，休繕治之官，雖則不寧，而時雨自降。

translations.literal: ’ Therefore when Duke Xi of Lu suffered drought he corrected government and disciplined himself, lowered the bells hung in the counties, and rested the officers who carried out repairs; although there was still unrest, seasonal rain fell of itself.
translations.idiomatic: So Duke Xi of Lu, facing drought, reformed himself, silenced county bells, and halted repairs—rain followed though troubles lingered.

sentences.zh: 由此言之，天之應人，敏於景響。

translations.literal: Judged by this, Heaven’s response to man is swifter than shadow and echo.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven answers humanity faster than shadow or echo.

sentences.zh: 今月十七日戊午，徵日也，日加申，風從寅來，丑時而止。

translations.literal: Now on the seventeenth day of this month, wuwu, it was a zhi day; when the sun reached shen, wind came from yin and ceased at the chou hour.
translations.idiomatic: On this month’s seventeenth, a wuwu day marked the zhi phase; when the sun stood at shen, wind rose from yin and stopped in chou.

sentences.zh: 丑、寅、申皆徵也，不有火災，必當為旱。

translations.literal: Chou, yin, and shen are all zhi notes; if there is not fire disaster, there must be drought.
translations.idiomatic: Chou, yin, and shen align with the fire note—expect either blaze or drought.

sentences.zh: 願陛下校計繕修之費，永念百姓之勞，罷將作之官，減雕文之飾，損皰廚之饌，退宴私之樂。

translations.literal: Your subject hopes Your Majesty will reckon the cost of repairs, ever remember the people’s toil, dismiss the Director of Works, reduce carved ornament, cut back kitchen feasts, and retire private banquet pleasures.
translations.idiomatic: Please audit construction expenses, remember popular labor, shutter the works bureau, strip gaudy carving, shrink palace kitchens, and end private revels.

sentences.zh: 《易中孚傳》曰：『陽感天，不旋日。

translations.literal: The Commentary on the Hexagram Zhongfu states: ‘When yang stirs Heaven, not a full day turns.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhongfu commentary promises: “When yang stirs Heaven, change comes within a day.”

sentences.zh: 』如是，則景雲降集，眚沴息矣。

translations.literal: ’ If it is so, then auspicious clouds will gather and baleful prodigies will rest.
translations.idiomatic: Do this and auspicious clouds will gather while ill omens cease.

sentences.zh: 二事：去年以來，《兌卦》用事，類多不效。

translations.literal: Second matter: since last year the hexagram Dui has administered affairs, yet kindred phenomena have mostly failed to appear.
translations.idiomatic: Second: since last year the Dui hexagram has governed, yet matching signs seldom appear.

sentences.zh: 《易傳》曰：『有貌無實，佞人也；

translations.literal: The Commentary on the Changes states: ‘Appearance without substance is the flatterer;
translations.idiomatic: The Changes commentary says: “Show without substance—that is the sycophant.”

sentences.zh: 有實無貌，道人也。

translations.literal: substance without appearance is the man of the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Substance without display—that is the true man of the Way.”

sentences.zh: 』寒溫為實，清濁為貌。

translations.literal: ’ Cold and warmth are substance; clarity and turbidity are appearance.
translations.idiomatic: Temperature is substance; clarity or murk is outward show.

sentences.zh: 今三公皆令色足恭，外厲內荏，以虛事上，無佐國之實，故清濁效而寒溫不效也，是以陰寒侵犯消息。

translations.literal: Now the Three Excellencies all have pleasing color and sufficient deference—severe outside, soft inside—serving superiors with emptiness and possessing no substance to aid the state; therefore clarity and turbidity take effect while cold and warmth do not, so yin cold encroaches on waxing and waning.
translations.idiomatic: Today’s three dukes look respectful yet hollow—stern in face, timid within—serving with empty show and no statecraft; omens of purity shift while seasons do not, so yin cold disrupts the cycle.

sentences.zh: 占曰：『日乘則有妖風，日蒙則有地裂。

translations.literal: Divination states: ‘When the sun rides high there are prodigious winds; when the sun is veiled there are earth fissures.
translations.idiomatic: Prognostication warns: “High sun brings freak winds; veiled sun splits earth.”

sentences.zh: 』如是三年，則致日食，陰侵其陽，漸積所致。

translations.literal: ’ If it is like this for three years, then solar eclipses will occur—yin encroaching on yang, gradually accumulated.
translations.idiomatic: Three years of this breed eclipses—yin eating yang, built layer on layer.

sentences.zh: 立春前後溫氣應節者，詔令寬也。

translations.literal: When warm qi matches the season before and after the Beginning of Spring, it means edicts and commands are lenient.
translations.idiomatic: If spring warmth arrives on schedule, your decrees are genuinely mild.

sentences.zh: 其後復寒者，無寬之實也。

translations.literal: When afterward it turns cold again, it means there is no substance to the leniency.
translations.idiomatic: If cold returns, the mercy was cosmetic.

sentences.zh: 夫十室之邑，必有忠信，率土之人，豈無貞賢，未聞朝廷有所賞拔，非所以求善贊務，弘濟元元。

translations.literal: In a hamlet of ten houses there must be loyalty and faithfulness; across the land’s people, how could there be no chaste worthies? Yet your subject has not heard of the court promoting any—this is not the way to seek goodness, assist tasks, and broadly save the common folk.
translations.idiomatic: Even ten households yield loyal men; surely the empire hides talent—yet none win recognition. That cannot nurture virtue or save the people.

sentences.zh: 宜采納良臣，以助聖化。

translations.literal: It is fitting to gather and employ good ministers to assist sagely transformation.
translations.idiomatic: Adopt worthy aides to advance sagely reform.

sentences.zh: 三事：臣聞天道不遠，三五復反。

translations.literal: Third matter: your subject has heard the Way of Heaven is not distant—the patterns of three and five return again.
translations.idiomatic: Third: Heaven’s pattern is near—the cycles of three and five repeat.

sentences.zh: 今年少陽之歲，法當乘起，恐後年已往，將遂驚動，涉歷天門，災成戊巳。

translations.literal: This year is the Lesser Yang year; by rule it should rise in succession; I fear that after the coming yi year turmoil will follow, crossing the Celestial Gate and calamity completing in wu and ji.
translations.idiomatic: This shaoyang year should build momentum; I fear after yi-year shocks will cross Heaven’s Gate and disaster climax in wu-ji.

sentences.zh: 今春當旱，夏必有水，臣以六日七分候之可知。

translations.literal: This spring ought to be droughty; summer must bring floods—I can know this by applying the six-day seven-part method.
translations.idiomatic: Spring will parch; summer will flood—the six-day-seven-part calendar proves it.

sentences.zh: 未災眚之來，緣類而應。

translations.literal: Before disasters arrive, they accord with their categories and respond.
translations.idiomatic: Before calamity strikes, omens align by category.

sentences.zh: 行有玷缺，則氣逆於天，精感變出，以戒人君。

translations.literal: When conduct shows flawed deficiency, then qi turns contrary in Heaven, essences stir and transform prodigies appear to warn the lord of men.
translations.idiomatic: Faulted conduct reverses celestial qi, stirs essences into omens, and warns the throne.

sentences.zh: 王者之義，時有不登，則損滋徹膳。

translations.literal: In the king’s duty, when seasons sometimes fail, he reduces kitchens and limits fare.
translations.idiomatic: True kings, when harvests fail, thin palace kitchens and skip delicacies.

sentences.zh: 數年以來，穀收稍減，家貧戶饉，歲不如昔。

translations.literal: For several years running grain harvests have slightly declined; families are poor and households hungry—each year is worse than the last.
translations.idiomatic: Years of meager harvests have left homes hungry—each harvest poorer than the last.

sentences.zh: 百姓不足，君誰與足？

translations.literal: When the common people are insufficient, for whom is the lord sufficient?
translations.idiomatic: When the people lack sufficiency, how can the ruler claim plenty?

sentences.zh: 水旱之災，雖尚未至，然君子遠覽，防微慮萌。

translations.literal: As for flood and drought disasters, although they have not yet arrived, the gentleman looks far, guards the subtle, and considers what sprouts.
translations.idiomatic: Though drought and flood have not struck, the wise ruler looks ahead and checks threats at their birth.

sentences.zh: 《老子》曰：『人之饑也，以其上食稅之多也。

translations.literal: Laozi states: ‘The people starve because those above eat away too much tax grain.
translations.idiomatic: Laozi says: “The people hunger because their superiors tax too heavily.”

sentences.zh: 』故孝文皇帝綈袍革舄，木器無文，約身薄賦，時致升平。

translations.literal: ’ Therefore Emperor Wen wore coarse silk and leather slippers; wooden vessels bore no pattern; he restrained himself and lightened levies, and the age rose to great peace.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen wore plain robes and leather shoes, kept utensils unadorned, tightened his own wants and cut taxes—peace followed.

sentences.zh: 今陛下聖德中興，宜遵前典，惟節惟約，天下幸甚。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty’s sagely virtue has revived the house of Han; you ought to follow the ancient models, practicing only restraint and economy—the empire would be profoundly blessed.
translations.idiomatic: Your reign renews Han’s virtue; hold to the old standards with austerity and thrift, and the realm will rejoice.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：『天道無親，常與善人。

translations.literal: The Book of Changes says: ‘The Way of Heaven shows no partiality; it ever sides with the good.
translations.idiomatic: The Changes teaches: “Heaven’s Way is impartial—it always aids the good.”

sentences.zh: 』是故高宗以享福，宋景以延年。

translations.literal: ’ Hence Gaozong thereby received blessing, and Duke Jing of Song thereby lengthened his years.
translations.idiomatic: So King Wu Ding won fortune, and Duke Jing of Song won long life.

sentences.zh: 四事：臣竊見皇子未立，儲宮無主，仰觀天文，太子不明。

translations.literal: Fourth matter: your subject has observed in private that no imperial son has been installed, the heir’s quarters stand without a master, and when one scans the sky the crown prince’s station is obscure.
translations.idiomatic: Fourth: no prince has been named heir; the Eastern Palace sits empty; the stars give no clear sign of an heir.

sentences.zh: 熒惑以去年春分後十六日在婁五度，推步《三統》，熒惑今當在翼九度，今反在柳三度，則不及五十餘度。

translations.literal: The Sparkling Deluder on the sixteenth day after last year’s spring equinox stood at five degrees in Bond; by the reckoning of the Triple Concordance calendar it ought now to lie at nine degrees in Wings, yet it instead sits at three degrees in Willow—more than fifty degrees short.
translations.idiomatic: Mars after last equinox sat five degrees in Bond; by the Triple Concordance it should now be nine degrees in Wings, yet it lingers in Willow—over fifty degrees behind prediction.

sentences.zh: 去年八月二十四日戊辰，熒惑歷輿鬼東入軒轅，出後星北，東去四度，北旋復還。

translations.literal: On the twenty-fourth of the eighth month last year, day wuchen, the Sparkling Deluder passed east through Ghost Cart into the Chariot, emerged north of the Rear Star, traveled east four degrees, then curved north and doubled back.
translations.idiomatic: Last year’s eighth month, day wuchen, Mars crossed Ghost Cart into the Chariot, slipped north of the Rear Star, jogged four degrees east, then swept north and returned.

sentences.zh: 軒轅者，後宮也。

translations.literal: The Chariot asterism is the harem.
translations.idiomatic: The Chariot signifies the inner palace.

sentences.zh: 熒惑者，至陽之精也，天之使也，而出入軒轅，繞還往來。

translations.literal: The Sparkling Deluder is the essence of utmost yang and Heaven’s envoy, yet it passes in and out of the Chariot, wheeling about.
translations.idiomatic: Mars embodies peak yang and acts as Heaven’s messenger—yet it shuttles through the harem stars.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：『天垂象，見吉凶。

translations.literal: The Book of Changes says: ‘Heaven hangs forth images; they reveal fortune and disaster.
translations.idiomatic: The Changes says: “Heaven displays signs—they disclose blessing or doom.”

sentences.zh: 』其意昭然可見矣。

translations.literal: ’ The intent is luminously plain.
translations.idiomatic: The meaning could hardly be clearer.

sentences.zh: 禮，天子一娶九女，嫡媵畢縣。

translations.literal: By ritual the Son of Heaven takes nine wives in one marriage; principal wife and attendants all observe regulated ranks.
translations.idiomatic: Rite allows the emperor one wedding with nine consorts, each rank properly ordered.

sentences.zh: 今宮人侍御，動以千計，或生而幽隔，人道不通，郁積之氣，上感皇天，故遣熒惑入軒轅，理人倫，垂象見異，以悟主上。

translations.literal: Today palace attendants number in the thousands; many live walled off from human contact from birth—blocked qi rises to stir August Heaven, therefore Heaven sends the Sparkling Deluder through the Chariot to reorder human relations and display strange signs to awaken the sovereign.
translations.idiomatic: Thousands of palace women languish cut off from normal life; pent-up grievance moves Heaven, so Mars trespasses the harem stars—warning you to restore kinship and heed the omen.

sentences.zh: 昔武王下車，出傾宮之女，表商容之間，以理人倫，以表賢德，故天授以聖子，成王是也。

translations.literal: Once King Wu stepped from his chariot he freed the Tilted Palace women and honored Shang Rong’s lane—thus ordering human relations and displaying worthy virtue—so Heaven granted him a sage heir in King Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: King Wu dismissed the harem captives and honored worthy men; Heaven answered with the sage heir King Cheng.

sentences.zh: 今陛下多積宮人，以違天意，故皇胤多夭，嗣體莫寄。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty crowds the inner palace against Heaven’s will, so imperial sons die young and the succession nowhere lodges.
translations.idiomatic: You stock the harem beyond Heaven’s intent—hence royal children perish early and the throne lacks an heir.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：『敬天之怒，不敢戲豫。

translations.literal: The Odes say: ‘Stand in awe of Heaven’s wrath; do not trifle or idle.
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry warns: “Fear Heaven’s anger—never mock or slacken.”

sentences.zh: 』方今之福，莫若廣嗣，廣嗣之術，可不深思？

translations.literal: ’ Of blessings today none surpasses multiplying heirs; can the art of multiplying heirs go unpondered?
translations.idiomatic: No blessing now exceeds abundant heirs; will you not weigh how to secure them?

sentences.zh: 宜簡出宮女恣其姻嫁，則天自降福，子孫千億。

translations.literal: You should release selected palace women to marry as they wish—Heaven will then send blessing and descendants beyond count.
translations.idiomatic: Dismiss surplus women to wed freely; Heaven will bless you with countless descendants.

sentences.zh: 惟陛下丁寧再三，留神於此。

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty repeat your earnest charge and fix your mind on this.
translations.idiomatic: I beg you dwell on this counsel again and again.

sentences.zh: 左右貴幸，亦宜惟臣之言，以悟陛下。

translations.literal: Those favored at your side should likewise ponder your subject’s words to enlighten Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: Your intimate attendants should echo these warnings until they reach you.

sentences.zh: 蓋善言古者合於今，善言天者合於人。

translations.literal: Those who speak the past well match the present; those who speak Heaven well match mankind.
translations.idiomatic: Sound history illumines today; sound astronomy illumines human affairs.

sentences.zh: 願訪問百僚，有違臣言者，臣當受茍言之罪。

translations.literal: I beg you poll the officials—if any disprove me, let me suffer punishment for rash speech.
translations.idiomatic: Question the bureaucracy; if I am wrong, punish me for reckless counsel.

sentences.zh: 五事：臣竊見去年閏月十七日已醜夜，有白氣從西方天苑趨左足，入玉井，數日乃滅。

translations.literal: Fifth matter: your subject observed that on the night of intercalary month’s seventeenth day, jichou, white vapors ran from the Western Celestial Park toward the Left Foot and entered Jade Well, vanishing after several days.
translations.idiomatic: Fifth: on jichou night of last year’s leap month, a white streak ran from the western park through the Left Foot into Jade Well and faded after days.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》曰：『有星孛於大辰。

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn Annals states: ‘A comet appeared in the Great Marker.
translations.idiomatic: The Annals records: “A broom star blazed in the Great Asterism.”

sentences.zh: 大辰者何？

translations.literal: What is the Great Marker?
translations.idiomatic: What counts as the Great Marker?

sentences.zh: 大火也。

translations.literal: It is Great Fire.
translations.idiomatic: It means the Great Fire star.

sentences.zh: 大火為大辰，伐又為大辰，北極亦為大辰。

translations.literal: Great Fire is the Great Marker; Net is also the Great Marker; the Pole Star is also the Great Marker.
translations.idiomatic: Great Fire, the Net constellation, and the pole—all serve as that great marker.

sentences.zh: 』所以孛一宿而連三宿者，言北辰王者之宮也。

translations.literal: ’ The comet brushed one lodge yet linked three because the North Star is the ruler’s celestial palace.
translations.idiomatic: One comet touching three lodges marks the polar palace of the true king.

sentences.zh: 凡中宮無節，政教亂逆，威武衰微，則此三星以應之也。

translations.literal: Whenever the inner palace lacks discipline, government runs wild, and martial majesty fades, these three stars answer.
translations.idiomatic: When the palace loses restraint, policy inverts, and military awe fails—the three markers flash response.

sentences.zh: 罰者白虎，其宿主兵，其國趙、魏，變見西方，亦應三輔。

translations.literal: The punisher is the White Tiger; its lodge governs weapons; its lands are Zhao and Wei; the anomaly showed westward and also corresponds to the Three Adjuncts.
translations.idiomatic: White Tiger rules war over Zhao, Wei, and the western capital region—the sign appeared there.

sentences.zh: 凡金氣為變，發在秋節。

translations.literal: Whenever metal qi shifts prodigiously, it erupts in autumn.
translations.idiomatic: Metal omens surface in fall.

sentences.zh: 臣恐立秋以後，趙、魏、關西將有羌寇畔戾之患。

translations.literal: Your subject fears that after Autumn Begins Zhao, Wei, and the lands west of the passes will suffer Qiang raids and rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: I fear Qiang trouble along Zhao, Wei, and Guanzhong once autumn opens.

sentences.zh: 宜豫宣告諸郡，使敬授人時，輕徭役，薄賦斂，勿妄繕起，堅倉獄，備守衛，回選賢能，以鎮撫之。

translations.literal: You should issue advance orders to every commandery to honor the farming seasons, lighten corvée and taxes, avoid rash construction, strengthen granaries and prisons, ready defenses, rotate worthy officers, and pacify the regions.
translations.idiomatic: Warn every district early: respect harvest times, cut labor and levies, halt needless building, fortify stores and jails, post guards, and assign able men to calm the west.

sentences.zh: 金精之變，責歸上司。

translations.literal: The transformation of metal’s essence—responsibility rests with superiors.
translations.idiomatic: Metal’s ill omen indicts those above.

sentences.zh: 宜以五月丙午，遣太尉服幹戚，建井，書玉板之策，引白氣之異，於西郊責躬求愆，謝咎皇天，消滅妖氣。

translations.literal: On bingwu of the fifth month send the Grand Commandant bearing shield and axe, raise the well-banner, inscribe the jade-tablet vow, cite the white-vapor prodigy, reproach yourself at the western suburb, apologize to Heaven, and purge malign qi.
translations.idiomatic: On the designated bingwu day dispatch the commander with ritual arms, record the covenant on jade, acknowledge the white qi sign at the western altar, and expunge baleful influence through fire-over-metal symbolism.

sentences.zh: 蓋以火勝金，轉禍為福也。

translations.literal: This uses fire to overcome metal and turns calamity into blessing.
translations.idiomatic: Fire conquering metal converts peril into fortune.

sentences.zh: 六事：臣竊見今月十四日乙卯巳時，白虹貫日。

translations.literal: Sixth matter: your subject observed that on this month’s fourteenth day yimao at the si hour a white arc spanned the sun.
translations.idiomatic: Sixth: at si hour on yimao the fourteenth, a white rainbow pierced the solar disk.

sentences.zh: 凡日傍氣色白而純者名為虹。

translations.literal: Whenever aura beside the sun is uniformly white it is termed a rainbow.
translations.idiomatic: Pure white solar halo counts as this “rainbow.”

sentences.zh: 貫日中者，侵太陽也；

translations.literal: One that pierces the sun encroaches on the Great Yang;
translations.idiomatic: A halo that cuts the disk means yin is encroaching on the great yang.

sentences.zh: 見於春者，政變常也。

translations.literal: When it appears in spring, government has departed from the norm.
translations.idiomatic: An appearance in spring means policy has strayed from the proper course.

sentences.zh: 方今中官外司，各各考事，其所考者，或非急務。

translations.literal: Today inner eunuchs and outer offices endlessly conduct investigations; what they investigate is not always urgent.
translations.idiomatic: Court eunuchs and ministries keep grilling cases—often trivial ones.

sentences.zh: 又恭陵火災，主名未立，多所收捕，備經考毒。

translations.literal: Further, after the Gongling fire no culprit was fixed; many were seized and tortured cruelly.
translations.idiomatic: Since the imperial tomb fire, unnamed blame has driven mass arrests and brutal interrogation.

sentences.zh: 尋火為天戒，以悟人君，可順而不可違，可敬而不可慢。

translations.literal: Remember fire is Heaven’s warning to awaken rulers—it must be obeyed, not flouted; revered, not scorned.
translations.idiomatic: Fire warns the throne: heed it, never dismiss it.

sentences.zh: 陛下宜恭已內省，以備後災。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought to humble yourself in reflection to guard against further disaster.
translations.idiomatic: Withdraw into scruple and self-scrutiny to forestall more omens.

sentences.zh: 凡諸考察，並須立秋。

translations.literal: All such investigations must await Autumn Begins.
translations.idiomatic: Defer harsh inquiries until autumn trials.

sentences.zh: 又《易傳》曰：『公能其事，序賢進士，後必有喜。

translations.literal: The Commentary on the Changes also says: ‘When the lord fulfills his tasks, ranks worthies and promotes scholars, joy follows.
translations.idiomatic: The Yi commentary promises joy when rulers elevate talent in due order.

sentences.zh: 』反之，則白虹貫日。

translations.literal: ’ The contrary is the white rainbow through the sun.
translations.idiomatic: Neglect that and you get the white rainbow across the sun.

sentences.zh: 以甲乙見者，則譴在中臺。

translations.literal: When it appears on jia or yi days the reproof falls on the Middle Terrace.
translations.idiomatic: Appearances on jia/yi days implicate the central ministry.

sentences.zh: 自司徒居位，陰陽多謬，久無虛已進賢之策，天下興議，異人同咨。

translations.literal: Since the Minister of Education took office yin and yang slip constantly; long there has been no humble search for worthies—the empire murmurs as one.
translations.idiomatic: Under the current minister cosmic order errs and talent goes ignored; popular outrage everywhere agrees.

sentences.zh: 且立春以來，金氣再見，金能勝木，必有兵氣，宜黜司徒以應天意。

translations.literal: Moreover since spring began metal qi has appeared twice; metal overcomes wood—war qi stirs—so dismiss the Minister of Education to answer Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Metal doubled since spring—metal cuts wood, auguring arms—remove the minister to placate Heaven.

sentences.zh: 陛下不早攘之，將負臣言，遺患百姓。

translations.literal: If Your Majesty delays removing him you will belie your servant’s warning and bequeath harm to the people.
translations.idiomatic: Delay will prove my counsel false and leave the commoners suffering.

sentences.zh: 七事：臣伏惟漢興以來三百三十九歲。

translations.literal: Seventh matter: your subject reflects that since Han arose three hundred thirty-nine years have passed.
translations.idiomatic: Seventh: the Han house has stood three hundred thirty-nine years.

sentences.zh: 於《詩三基》，高祖起亥仲二年，今在戌仲十年。

translations.literal: In the Three Foundations of the Odes Gaozu rose in the second hai-central year; we now stand in the tenth xu-central year.
translations.idiomatic: By the “Three Foundations” chronology Gaozu began in hai zhong 2; we are now xu zhong 10.

sentences.zh: 《詩汜歷樞》曰：『卯酉為革政，午亥為革命，神在天門，出入候聽。

translations.literal: The Pivot of the Flooding Calendar of the Odes states: ‘Mao and you mark administrative revolution; wu and hai mark dynastic revolution; the spirit stands at Heaven’s Gate, going forth and returning to listen.
translations.idiomatic: Apocrypha says mao/you and wu/hai mark revolutions; spirits at Heaven’s Gate watch and listen.

sentences.zh: 』言神在戌亥，司候帝王興衰得失，厥善則昌，厥惡則亡。

translations.literal: ’ It means the spirit in xu and hai oversees royal rise and fall—good brings flourishing, evil brings ruin.
translations.idiomatic: Spirits stationed in xu/hai watch each reign’s merit—virtue thrives, vice dies.

sentences.zh: 於《易雄雌秘曆》，今值困乏。

translations.literal: In the Male-Female Secret Calendar of the Changes we now meet the hexagram Kun’s exhaustion.
translations.idiomatic: The secret Yi calendar places us in Kun’s “trapped” phase.

sentences.zh: 凡九二困者，眾小人欲共困害君子也。

translations.literal: Generally Kun’s nine-second line means a mob of petty men conspire to trap the gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Kun’s second yang pictures petty men besieging their betters.

sentences.zh: 《經》曰：『困而不失其所，其唯君子乎！

translations.literal: The Classic says: ‘Trapped yet not losing his ground—is it not the gentleman alone?
translations.idiomatic: The canon asks: “Trapped yet steadfast—is this not the gentleman?”

sentences.zh: 』唯獨賢聖之君，遭困遇險，能致命遂誌，不去其道。

translations.literal: ’ Only sage rulers, trapped in peril, can stake life on purpose without forsaking the Way.
translations.idiomatic: True kings endure danger yet honor their mandate.

sentences.zh: 陛下乃者潛龍養德，幽隱屈厄，即位之元，紫宮驚動，歷運之會，時氣已應。

translations.literal: Your Majesty once was the hidden dragon nurturing virtue in obscurity and hardship; in your accession year the Purple Palace trembled—the cyclical conjunction already responded.
translations.idiomatic: You formerly cultivated virtue in concealment; omens stirred your first year—the cosmic pivot already shifted.

sentences.zh: 然猶恐妖祥未盡，君子思患而豫防之。

translations.literal: Still one fears auspicious and baleful signs are not exhausted; the gentleman ponders peril beforehand.
translations.idiomatic: Yet omens may remain; wise rulers anticipate disaster early.

sentences.zh: 臣以為戌仲已竟，來年入季，文帝改法，除肉刑之罪，至今適三百載。

translations.literal: Your subject believes xu-central has closed and next year enters the final third; Emperor Wen’s reform abolishing flesh punishments was exactly three hundred years ago.
translations.idiomatic: Xu zhong ends as we enter the final third; three centuries have passed since Wen curtailed corporal law.

sentences.zh: 宜因斯際，大蠲法令，官名稱號，輿服器械，事有所更，變大為小，去奢就儉，機衡之政，除煩為簡。

translations.literal: Seize the moment to remit statutes wholesale—titles, badges, vehicles, gear—alter what must be altered, trade grandeur for modesty, extravagance for thrift, and replace tangled administration with simplicity.
translations.idiomatic: Use the turn to slash redundant laws, retitle offices, simplify regalia, shrink ritual excess, and streamline the pivot of government.

sentences.zh: 改元更始，招求幽隱，舉方正，徵有道，博采異謀，開不諱之路。

translations.literal: Change the era name and begin anew; summon recluses; promote the forthright and the possessed-of-the-Way; gather diverse counsel; open frank speech.
translations.idiomatic: Proclaim a new reign year, call hidden worthies, recruit the upright, invite debate, and welcome blunt advice.

sentences.zh: 臣陳引際會，恐犯忌諱，書不盡言，未敢究暢。

translations.literal: Your subject cited these convergences but feared taboos; the writing cannot say everything, nor dare expand fully.
translations.idiomatic: I cited these turns timidly—taboos bind me from saying more.

sentences.zh: 臺詰顗曰：『對云「白虹貫日，政變常也。

translations.literal: The Terrace questioned Yi: ‘Your reply said “White rainbow through the sun—government departs from the norm.”
translations.idiomatic: Censors asked: “You said the spring rainbow meant policy was irregular.”

sentences.zh: 」朝廷率由舊章，何所變易而言變常？

translations.literal: ’ The court wholly follows old statutes—what changed that you call it irregular?
translations.idiomatic: They protested that court follows precedent—what changed?

sentences.zh: 又言「當大蠲法令，革易官號」。

translations.literal: Further you said “ought greatly to remit laws and change office titles.”
translations.idiomatic: They quoted your demand to overhaul statutes and titles.

sentences.zh: 或云變常以致災，或改舊以除異，何也？

translations.literal: Some say altering the norm brings disaster; others say changing the old removes anomalies—which is it?
translations.idiomatic: Some link irregular policy to omens; others say reform fixes them—which holds?

sentences.zh: 又陽嘉初建，復欲改元，據何經典？

translations.literal: Further Yangjia was just begun—again wishing to change the era name—on what authority?
translations.idiomatic: They challenged a second era change so soon after Yangjia.

sentences.zh: 其以實對。

translations.literal: Answer with concrete facts.
translations.idiomatic: They demanded plain proof.

sentences.zh: 』顗對曰：

translations.literal: ’ Yi replied:
translations.idiomatic: Lang Yi answered:

sentences.zh: 方春東作，布德之元，陽氣開發，養導萬物。

translations.literal: It is spring’s eastern labors, the hour of spreading virtue; yang opens and nurtures the myriad things.
translations.idiomatic: Spring’s eastern work awakens yang and fosters creation.

sentences.zh: 王者因天視聽，奉順時氣，宜務崇溫柔，尊其行令。

translations.literal: The king mirrors Heaven’s sight and hearing, obeys seasonal qi, ought to prize gentleness and honor timely commands.
translations.idiomatic: Sovereigns echo Heaven—spring calls for mild rule and seasonal justice.

sentences.zh: 而今立春之後，考事不息，秋冬之政，行乎春夏，故白虹春見，掩蔽日曜。

translations.literal: Yet after the Beginning of Spring investigations continue without cease—autumn and winter harshness runs through spring and summer; hence the white rainbow in spring veils the sun.
translations.idiomatic: Yet punishments roll on in spring—autumn severity in spring skies—hence the rainbow across the sun.

sentences.zh: 凡邪氣乘陽，則虹霓在日，斯皆臣下執事刻急所致。

translations.literal: Whenever evil qi rides yang, rainbows cling to the sun—all from underlings enforcing law with cruelty.
translations.idiomatic: Yin riding yang paints halos—because clerks torment the people.

sentences.zh: 殆非朝廷優寬之本。

translations.literal: Hardly does this match the court’s ideal of lenience.
translations.idiomatic: That is not the mercy the throne should show.

sentences.zh: 此其變常之咎也。

translations.literal: This is the fault called departing from the norm.
translations.idiomatic: That is the “irregular government” I meant.

sentences.zh: 又今選舉皆歸三司，非有周、召之才，而當則哲之重，每有選用，輒參之掾屬，公府門巷，賓客填集，送去迎來，財貨無已。

translations.literal: Further, selection now rests with the Three Bureaus—they lack the caliber of the Duke of Zhou and Duke of Shao yet bear the duty of judging talent; every appointment consults clerks; bureau lanes swarm with guests, endless farewells and welcomes, bribes without cease.
translations.idiomatic: Personnel power sits with the three offices—not Zhou-level sages—yet every hire runs through clerks, corridors clog with favor-seekers, and gifts never stop.

sentences.zh: 其當遷者，競相薦謁，各遣子弟，充塞道路，開長奸門，興致浮偽，非所謂率由舊章也。

translations.literal: Those due promotion vie to petition; each sends sons and brothers until roads clog—opening wide gates to deceit and puffery—this is not following old statutes.
translations.idiomatic: Candidates flood recommendations with kin—breeding fraud—hardly “following the models.”

sentences.zh: 尚書職在機衡，宮禁嚴密，私曲之意，羌不得通，偏黨之恩，或無所用。

translations.literal: The Masters of Writing hold the cosmic pivot; palace security is tight—private bias cannot penetrate, partisan favor finds little room.
translations.idiomatic: The secretariat sits at the hinge—sealed against graft.

sentences.zh: 選舉之任，不如還在機密。

translations.literal: Selection duty is better returned to that confidential office.
translations.idiomatic: Let appointments return to the Masters of Writing.

sentences.zh: 臣誠愚戇，不知折中，斯固遠近之論，當今之宜。

translations.literal: Your subject is dull and blunt, knows no golden mean—yet near and far agree this suits today.
translations.idiomatic: I am crude, yet consensus says this fits the age.

sentences.zh: 又孔子曰：『漢三百載，鬥歷改憲。

translations.literal: Further Confucius said: ‘When Han reaches three hundred years the Dipper calendar changes the constitution.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius predicted Han’s calendar shift at three hundred years.

sentences.zh: 』三百四歲為一德，五德千五百二十歲，五行更用。

translations.literal: ’ Three hundred four years form one virtue-phase; five virtues span one thousand five hundred twenty years; the five phases rotate.
translations.idiomatic: Each virtue-cycle lasts 304 years; five cycles span 1,520—elements take turns.

sentences.zh: 王者隨天，譬猶自春徂夏，改青服絳者也。

translations.literal: Kings follow Heaven as one swaps green spring robes for red summer ones.
translations.idiomatic: Sovereigns track Heaven the way seasons swap colors.

sentences.zh: 自文帝省刑，適三百年，而輕微之禁，漸已殷積。

translations.literal: Since Emperor Wen softened punishments three hundred years have passed, yet petty prohibitions have piled deep.
translations.idiomatic: Three centuries after Wen’s mercy code, petty bans still accumulate.

sentences.zh: 王者之法，譬猶江河，當使易避而難犯也。

translations.literal: The king’s law should resemble rivers—easy to avoid, hard to breach.
translations.idiomatic: Royal law should flow like a river—easy to steer clear of, hard to violate.

sentences.zh: 故《易》曰：『易則易知，簡則易從，易簡而天下之理得矣。

translations.literal: Therefore the Changes says: ‘Easy, then easy to understand; simple, then easy to follow; easy and simple—then the realm’s pattern is grasped.
translations.idiomatic: The Changes praises clarity: simple laws reveal Heaven’s pattern.

sentences.zh: 』今去奢即儉，以先天下，改易名號，隨事稱謂。

translations.literal: ’ Now reject extravagance for thrift to lead the realm; alter names and titles case by case.
translations.idiomatic: Lead by austerity, rename offices to match reality.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：『君子之道，或出或處，同歸殊塗，一致百慮。

translations.literal: The Changes says: ‘The gentleman’s path—sometimes out, sometimes in; same end by different roads; one purpose, many thoughts.
translations.idiomatic: The sage’s way exits and enters—many routes, one goal.

sentences.zh: 』是知變常而善，可以除災，變常而惡，必致於異。

translations.literal: ’ Thus know: changing course virtuously removes disaster; changing course viciously breeds prodigy.
translations.idiomatic: Reform done right ends calamity; done wrong summons omens.

sentences.zh: 今年仲竟，來年入季，仲終季始，歷運變改，故可改元，所以順天道也。

translations.literal: This year’s middle third closes; next year enters the final third—mid ends as late begins; the calendar turns—hence a new era name obeys Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The cycle’s pivot invites a new reign motto—aligning with cosmic timing.

sentences.zh: 臣顗愚蔽，不足以答聖問。

translations.literal: Your subject Yi is foolish and blocked—unable to exhaust sagely questions.
translations.idiomatic: I lack wit to answer every royal query.

sentences.zh: 顗又上書薦黃瓊、李固，並陳消災之術曰：

translations.literal: Yi again memorialized recommending Huang Qiong and Li Gu, and stated techniques for dispelling disaster:
translations.idiomatic: Lang Yi next recommended Huang Qiong and Li Gu and outlined remedies:

sentences.zh: 臣前對七事，要政急務，宜於今者，所當施用。

translations.literal: Your subject’s earlier reply on seven matters listed urgent policies suited to the moment—those deserve enactment.
translations.idiomatic: The seven measures I offered fit present needs.

sentences.zh: 誠知愚淺，不合聖聽，人賤言廢，當受誅罰，徵營惶怖，靡知厝身。

translations.literal: Truly knowing my shallowness cannot match sagely hearing—when men are low their words are cast aside; I ought to suffer punishment; anxious and trembling, I know nowhere to place myself.
translations.idiomatic: I risk execution for blunt counsel and tremble without refuge.

sentences.zh: 臣聞刳舟剡楫，將欲濟江海也；

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that hollowing boats and carving oars aims to cross rivers and seas;
translations.idiomatic: One hollows hulls and trims oars to ride the rivers and seas;

sentences.zh: 聘賢選佐，將以安天下也。

translations.literal: engaging worthies and selecting aides aims to pacify the realm.
translations.idiomatic: one hires worthies and picks aides to bring peace to the realm.

sentences.zh: 昔唐堯在上，群龍為用，文、武創德，周、召作輔，是以能建天地之功，增日月之耀者也。

translations.literal: Formerly Tang Yao above employed the host of dragons; Wen and Wu founded virtue; Zhou and Shao assisted—therefore they could establish Heaven-and-Earth merit and magnify sun-and-moon brilliance.
translations.idiomatic: Yao deployed many dragons; Wen, Wu, Zhou, and Shao built dynastic glory.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云：『赫赫王命，仲山甫將之。

translations.literal: The Odes say: ‘Awesome was the king’s command—Zhong Shanfu carried it out.
translations.idiomatic: The Poetry cries: “Sovereign majesty—Zhong Shanfu enacted it.”

sentences.zh: 邦國若否，仲山甫明之。

translations.literal: Whether the states were well-governed or not, Zhong Shanfu clarified it.
translations.idiomatic: How the regional states fared—Zhong Shanfu read each case aright.

sentences.zh: 』宣王是賴，以致雍熙。

translations.literal: ’ King Xuan relied on him, thereby reaching harmonious brilliance.
translations.idiomatic: King Xuan leaned on him, and good order followed.

sentences.zh: 陛下踐祚以來，勤心庶政，而三九之位，未見其人，是以災害屢臻，四國未寧。

translations.literal: Since Your Majesty mounted the throne you have toiled at myriad policies, yet in the highest offices no suitable men appear—therefore disasters arrive repeatedly and the four quarters know no peace.
translations.idiomatic: Your reign has been diligent, yet the chief posts still lack the right men—hence omens pile up and the realm stays unsettled.

sentences.zh: 臣考之國典，驗之聞見，莫不以得賢為功，失士為敗。

translations.literal: Your subject has examined the dynastic statutes and tested them against what he has heard and seen: none fail to take winning worthies as achievement and losing scholars as defeat.
translations.idiomatic: The archives and my own observation agree—states thrive by gaining talent and fail by losing it.

sentences.zh: 且賢者出處，翔而後集，爵以德進，則其情不茍，然後使君子恥貧賤而樂富貴矣。

translations.literal: Moreover worthies in leaving or serving hover and then settle; if rank advances through virtue, their intent is not careless—then the gentleman is ashamed of poverty and baseness and delights in wealth and honor.
translations.idiomatic: The worthy hesitate before serving; when promotion tracks merit, gentlemen gladly trade poverty for honorable station.

sentences.zh: 若有德不報，有言不酬，來無所樂，進無所趨，則皆懷歸藪澤，修其故誌矣。

translations.literal: If there is virtue yet no reward, words yet no response, no joy in coming forward and no goal in advancing, then all will think of returning to rivers and marshes and cultivating their old resolve.
translations.idiomatic: If merit goes unrewarded and counsel unheard, men drift home to the wilds and cling to private purpose.

sentences.zh: 夫求賢者，上以承天，下以為人。

translations.literal: In seeking worthies, above one answers Heaven; below one acts on behalf of the people.
translations.idiomatic: Seeking talent serves Heaven above and the people below.

sentences.zh: 不用之，則逆天統，違人望。

translations.literal: If you do not employ them, you oppose Heaven’s thread and betray popular expectation.
translations.idiomatic: To spurn them defies cosmic order and popular hope.

sentences.zh: 逆天統則災眚降，違人望則化不行。

translations.literal: Opposing Heaven’s thread brings calamities down; betraying popular expectation means transformation cannot proceed.
translations.idiomatic: The first invites disaster; the second blocks moral sway.

sentences.zh: 災眚降則下呼嗟，化不行則君道虧。

translations.literal: When calamities descend, the people below cry aloud; when transformation fails to proceed, the ruler’s way is damaged.
translations.idiomatic: Calamity breeds popular outcry; failed reform costs the throne its moral authority.

sentences.zh: 四始之缺，五際之厄，其咎由此。

translations.literal: The breach in the four beginnings, the misfortune of the five junctures—its blame arises from this.
translations.idiomatic: The breaks in the seasonal cycles—fault lies here.

sentences.zh: 豈可不剛健篤實，矜矜栗栗，以守天功盛德大業乎？

translations.literal: How can you fail to be firm, robust, and true, trembling with care, guarding Heaven’s great merit, great virtue, and great enterprise?
translations.idiomatic: You must stand steadfast and vigilant, guarding Heaven’s charge and the dynasty’s great work.

sentences.zh: 臣伏見光祿大夫江夏黃瓊，耽道樂術，清亮自然，被褐懷寶，含味經籍，又果於從政，明達變復。

translations.literal: Your subject has seen Grand Counsellor of the Household Huang Qiong of Jiangxia—he immerses himself in the Way and delights in its arts, clear and bright by nature, coarse-clad yet cherishing the gem, savoring the classics, resolute in governance, and discerning in change and response.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Qiong of Jiangxia—grand counselor—loves learning, stays humble while packing talent, knows the canon, governs decisively, and reads shifting signs.

sentences.zh: 朝廷前加優寵，賓於上位。

translations.literal: The court earlier showed him exceptional favor and seated him as guest of honor.
translations.idiomatic: The court once honored him and gave him a seat among the highest.

sentences.zh: 瓊入朝日淺，謀謨未就，因以喪病，致命遂誌。

translations.literal: Qiong’s tenure at court has been short; his plans are not yet complete; because of bereavement and illness he surrendered life to fulfill his purpose.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Qiong had little time at court; mourning and illness cut short his service before his designs ripened.

sentences.zh: 《老子》曰：『大音希聲，大器晚成。

translations.literal: Laozi says: ‘The great tone is thin in sound; the great vessel completes late.
translations.idiomatic: Laozi says the greatest voice is barely heard; the greatest vessel finishes late.

sentences.zh: 』善人為固，三年乃立。

translations.literal: ’ The good man’s work is sound—only after three years is it established.
translations.idiomatic: Solid virtue needs years to take root.

sentences.zh: 天下莫不嘉朝廷有此良人，而復怪其不時還任。

translations.literal: There is none in the realm who does not praise the court for having this good man, yet all marvel that he was not speedily returned to office.
translations.idiomatic: All praise the court for Huang Qiong—yet wonder why he was not brought back sooner.

sentences.zh: 陛下宜加降崇之恩，極養賢之禮，徵反京師，以慰天下。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought to add marks of honor, carry the rite of nurturing worthies to the utmost, summon him back to the capital, and console the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Raise his honors, fulfill the ritual of fostering talent, recall him to Luoyang, and reassure the empire.

sentences.zh: 又處士漢中李固，年四十，通遊、夏之藝，履顏、閔之仁。

translations.literal: Further, the recluse scholar Li Gu of Hanzhong, forty years old, has mastered the arts of Ziyou and Zixia and walks the humaneness of Yan Hui and Min Ziqian.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu of Hanzhong, forty, commands the disciples’ learning and the moral gravity of Yan and Min.

sentences.zh: 潔白之節，情同敫日，忠貞之操，好是正直，卓冠古人，當世莫及。

translations.literal: The stainless purity of his integrity rivals the bright sun; the constancy of his loyalty esteems the straight and upright—he towers above the ancients; none today can equal him.
translations.idiomatic: His integrity gleams like noon; his loyalty cleaves to the right—he outshines antiquity.

sentences.zh: 元精所生，王之佐臣，天之生固，必為聖漢，宜蒙特徵，以示四方。

translations.literal: Born of essential essence, he is a king’s supporting minister; Heaven’s producing Gu must be for sage Han—he ought to receive a special summons to signal the four quarters.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven bred Li Gu to aid sage Han—summon him specially as a sign to the realm.

sentences.zh: 夫有出倫之才，不應限以官次。

translations.literal: One whose talent exceeds the common norm ought not be bounded by official seniority.
translations.idiomatic: Extraordinary gifts must not be shackled to rank and seniority.

sentences.zh: 昔顏子十八，天下歸仁；

translations.literal: Formerly Yan Hui at eighteen had the realm turn to humaneness;
translations.idiomatic: Yan Hui at eighteen drew the world toward virtue;

sentences.zh: 子奇稚齒，化阿有聲。

translations.literal: Ziqi, still in youth, transformed E and left a reputation.
translations.idiomatic: the boy magistrate Ziqi reformed his district while scarcely grown.

sentences.zh: 若還瓊徵固，任以時政，伊尹、傅說，不足為此，則可垂景光，致休祥矣。

translations.literal: If you recall Qiong and summon Gu, entrusting them with current policy, not even Yi Yin or Fu Yue would surpass this—then you may let radiance hang down and bring propitious omens.
translations.idiomatic: Restore Huang Qiong, call Li Gu, and give them real power—rivals to Yi Yin and Fu Yue—and blessing will follow.

sentences.zh: 臣顗明不知人，伏聽眾言，百姓所歸，臧否共嘆。

translations.literal: Your subject Yi is not acute at judging men; I bow to popular judgment—the people flock to them; praise and blame sound as one.
translations.idiomatic: I am no judge of character, but the people sing one verdict on these men.

sentences.zh: 願泛問百僚，核其名行，有一不合，則臣為欺國。

translations.literal: I beg to question all officials broadly and verify names against conduct; if a single point fails, your subject has deceived the state.
translations.idiomatic: Poll the bureaucracy—if any charge fails, execute me for deceit.

sentences.zh: 惟留聖神，不以人廢言。

translations.literal: Only may the sage spirit not reject counsel because of the speaker’s low station.
translations.idiomatic: Do not dismiss truth for the messenger’s rank.

sentences.zh: 謹復條便宜四事，附奏於左：

translations.literal: Respectfully I list four further convenient measures, appending them to the memorial at left:
translations.idiomatic: I submit four more practical items below:

sentences.zh: 一事：孔子作《春秋》，書『正月』者，敬歲之始也。

translations.literal: First matter: Confucius composed the Spring and Autumn Annals; he wrote ‘first month’ to honor the year’s beginning.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius dated the Annals from the first month to sanctify the new year.

sentences.zh: 王者則天之象，因時之序，宜開發德號，爵賢命士，流寬大之澤，垂仁厚之德，順助元氣，含養庶類。

translations.literal: The king patterns himself on Heaven’s image and follows the seasons’ order; it is fit to issue a virtuous proclamation, ennoble the worthy and commission knights, spread lenient grace, extend generous virtue, assist the primordial qi, and embrace the myriad folk.
translations.idiomatic: Model Heaven’s rhythm: proclaim virtue, enfeoff talent, shower mercy, nurture vital qi, and shelter the people.

sentences.zh: 如此，則天文昭爛，星辰顯列，五緯循軌，四時和睦。

translations.literal: If so, celestial patterns will blaze, stars stand arrayed, the five cords follow their tracks, and the four seasons harmonize.
translations.idiomatic: Do this and the sky clears, planets align, and seasons cooperate.

sentences.zh: 不則太陽不光，天地混濁，時氣錯逆，霾霧蔽日。

translations.literal: If not, the great yang loses light, Heaven and Earth turn murky, seasonal qi runs contrary, and haze veils the sun.
translations.idiomatic: Otherwise the sun dulls, weather fouls, and smog masks daylight.

sentences.zh: 自立春以來，累經旬朔，未見仁德有所施布，但聞罪罰考掠之聲。

translations.literal: Since the Beginning of Spring many days have passed, yet I have seen no humaneness or virtue spread—only the sound of crimes, punishments, and torture.
translations.idiomatic: Since spring opened I hear only torture and sentence—not mercy.

sentences.zh: 夫天之應人，疾如景響，而自從入歲，常有蒙氣，月不舒光，日不宣曜。

translations.literal: Heaven answers man swifter than shadow or echo, yet since the new year shrouding qi has lingered, the moon has not spread its radiance, the sun has not blazed bright.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven reacts instantly, yet haze has veiled moon and sun since New Year.

sentences.zh: 日者太陽，以象人君，政變於下，日應於天。

translations.literal: The sun is the great yang; it images the lord of men—when policy shifts below, the sun responds above.
translations.idiomatic: The sun mirrors the throne—earthly misrule dims it.

sentences.zh: 清濁之占，隨政抑揚。

translations.literal: The reading of clear versus turbid tracks governance, rising or falling with it.
translations.idiomatic: Clear or murky solar omens track good and bad rule.

sentences.zh: 天之見異，事無虛作。

translations.literal: Heaven’s display of strangeness—events are not wrought without cause.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven’s signs never appear without reason.

sentences.zh: 豈獨陛下倦於萬機，帷幄之政有所闕歟？

translations.literal: Is it only that Your Majesty wearies of the myriad handles of state, and governance within the curtain is somewhat lacking?
translations.idiomatic: Are you worn by endless affairs while inner counsel falters?

sentences.zh: 何天戒之數見也！

translations.literal: Why do Heaven’s warnings appear so thickly!
translations.idiomatic: Why so many celestial warnings?

sentences.zh: 臣願陛下發揚乾剛，援引賢能，勤求機衡之寄，以獲斷金之利。

translations.literal: Your subject hopes Your Majesty will rouse Qian’s firmness, draw in the worthy and able, diligently seek the right men for the cosmic pivot, and gain the strength of metal severed in unity.
translations.idiomatic: Summon steadfast virtue, recruit talent, fill the ministry’s hinge posts, and forge united rule.

sentences.zh: 臣之所陳，輒以太陽為先者，明其不可久暗，急當改正。

translations.literal: The reason your subject lists the great sun first is to show that it cannot long stay dark and must urgently be corrected.
translations.idiomatic: I stress the sun because its eclipse cannot stand—you must reform now.

sentences.zh: 其異雖微，其事甚重。

translations.literal: Although the prodigy is slight, the matter is weighty indeed.
translations.idiomatic: Small omen, enormous stakes.

sentences.zh: 臣言雖約，其旨甚廣。

translations.literal: Although your subject’s words are brief, their purport is very broad.
translations.idiomatic: Few words, wide meaning.

sentences.zh: 惟陛下乃眷臣章，深留明思。

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty turn your regard to your subject’s memorial and ponder it with clear thought.
translations.idiomatic: Please study this memorial closely.

sentences.zh: 二事：孔子曰：『雷之始發《大壯》始，君弱臣強從《解》起。

translations.literal: Second matter: Confucius said: ‘When thunder first sounds it begins from Dazhuang; when the lord is weak and ministers strong, it begins from Jie.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius tied first thunder to hexagram Dazhuang and ministerial dominance to Jie.

sentences.zh: 』今月九日至十四日，《大壯》用事，消息之卦也。

translations.literal: ’ From the ninth to the fourteenth of this month Dazhuang administers affairs—it is the hexagram of waxing and waning.
translations.idiomatic: Days nine through fourteen belong to Dazhuang—the hexagram of rise and fall.

sentences.zh: 於此六日之中，雷當發聲，發聲則歲氣和，王道興也。

translations.literal: Within these six days thunder ought to sound; when it sounds the year’s qi is harmonious and the kingly Way ascends.
translations.idiomatic: Thunder should roll in that span—harmony of qi and ascent of the royal Way.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：『雷出地奮，豫，先王以作樂崇德，殷薦之上帝。

translations.literal: The Changes says: ‘Thunder comes from the earth, joyous—the former kings made music to honor virtue and presented rich offerings to High God.
translations.idiomatic: The Yi praises thunder from earth—sage kings answered it with ritual music.

sentences.zh: 』雷者，所以開發萌牙，辟陰除害。

translations.literal: ’ Thunder opens the sprouts, drives off yin, and removes harm.
translations.idiomatic: Thunder wakes sprouts and scourges harmful yin.

sentences.zh: 萬物須雷而解，資雨而潤。

translations.literal: The myriad things need thunder to be released and rain to be moistened.
translations.idiomatic: Creation needs thunder’s push and rain’s soak.

sentences.zh: 故《經》曰：『雷以動之，雨以潤之。

translations.literal: Therefore the classic says: ‘With thunder you stir them; with rain you moisten them.
translations.idiomatic: The canon pairs thunder’s stir with rain’s nurture.

sentences.zh: 』王者崇寬大，順春令，則雷應節，不則發動於冬，當震反潛。

translations.literal: ’ When kings esteem breadth and leniency and obey spring commands, thunder matches the season; if not, it fires in winter—where there should be quake it sinks away.
translations.idiomatic: Merciful spring rule draws timely thunder; cruelty drives thunder into winter silence.

sentences.zh: 故《易傳》曰：『當雷不雷，太陽弱也。

translations.literal: Therefore the Yi commentary says: ‘When thunder should sound yet does not, the great yang is weak.
translations.idiomatic: Missing thunder means weak sovereign yang.

sentences.zh: 』今蒙氣不除，日月變色，則其效也。

translations.literal: ’ Now shrouding qi is not cleared; sun and moon change color—that is its verification.
translations.idiomatic: Murky air still cloaks the luminaries—that proves the text.

sentences.zh: 天網恢恢，疏而不失，隨時進退，應政得失。

translations.literal: Heaven’s net is vast—loose yet nothing escapes; it advances and retreats with the times, answering governance’s hits and misses.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven’s mesh is wide yet catches all—omens track policy faithfully.

sentences.zh: 大人者，與天地合其德，與日月合其明，璇璣動作，與天相應。

translations.literal: The great man joins virtue with Heaven and Earth and brightness with sun and moon; the polar pivot turns and moves in accord with Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Great rulers mirror Heaven and rotate policy with the seasons.

sentences.zh: 雷者號令，其德生養。

translations.literal: Thunder is command and instruction; its virtue is to engender and nurture.
translations.idiomatic: Thunder embodies imperial command and the nurture of life.

sentences.zh: 號令殆廢，當生而殺，則雷反作，其時無歲。

translations.literal: When command and instruction are nearly stilled, when there should be nurture yet killing occurs, then thunder responds contrarily—at that season the year is lost.
translations.idiomatic: When edicts fail and life turns to slaughter, thunder answers awry and harvests fail.

sentences.zh: 陛下若欲除災昭祉，順天致和，宜察臣下尤酷害者，亟加斥黜，以安黎元，則太皓悅和，雷聲乃發。

translations.literal: If Your Majesty wishes to dispel disaster and show blessing, obey Heaven and bring harmony, you should inspect underlings for the cruelest and dismiss them promptly to console the people—then the White Sovereign will be pleased and harmonious, and thunder will sound.
translations.idiomatic: Purge cruel officials, ease the people, and thunder will roll as Heaven intends.

sentences.zh: 三事：去年十月二十日癸亥，太白與歲星合於房、心。

translations.literal: Third matter: last year on the twentieth of the tenth month, guihai, Great White and the Year Star joined in Fang and Xin.
translations.idiomatic: Third: last year’s tenth month, day guihai, Venus and Jupiter met in Fang and Xin.

sentences.zh: 太白在北，歲星在南，相離數寸，光芒交接。

translations.literal: Great White lay north, the Year Star south, only inches apart, their radiances crossing.
translations.idiomatic: Venus north, Jupiter south, their beams touching.

sentences.zh: 房、心者，天帝明堂布政之宮。

translations.literal: Fang and Xin are the palace where August Heaven spreads policy—the Bright Hall.
translations.idiomatic: Those lodges mark the celestial hall where policy is proclaimed.

sentences.zh: 《孝經鉤命決》曰：『歲星守心年穀豐。

translations.literal: The Xiaojing gou ming jue says: ‘When the Year Star guards the Heart, the year’s grain is abundant.
translations.idiomatic: Apocrypha promises bumper crops when Jupiter guards the Heart.

sentences.zh: 』《尚書洪範記》曰：『月行中道，移節應期，德厚受福，重華留之。

translations.literal: ’ The Hong Fan record in the Documents says: ‘The moon travels the central path, shifting nodes answering to season—thick virtue receives blessing; Chong Hua lingers there.
translations.idiomatic: The Documents’ Hong Fan notes lunar omens and Chong Hua’s halt.

sentences.zh: 』重華者，謂歲星在心也。

translations.literal: ’ Chong Hua refers to the Year Star in the Heart.
translations.idiomatic: “Chong Hua” names Jupiter in the Heart lodge.

sentences.zh: 今太白從之，交合明堂，金木相賊，而反同合，此以陰陵陽，臣下專權之異也。

translations.literal: ’ Now Great White follows it, joining in the Bright Hall—metal and wood attack each other, yet they unite against expectation—this is yin overwhelming yang, the prodigy of ministers monopolizing power.
translations.idiomatic: Venus joins Jupiter in the hall of state—metal and wood clash yet merge: yin ministers eclipse the sovereign.

sentences.zh: 房、心東方，其國主宋。

translations.literal: Fang and Xin lie east; their state chiefly is Song.
translations.idiomatic: Those eastern lodges align with Song.

sentences.zh: 《石氏經》曰：『歲星出左有年，出右無年。

translations.literal: The Shi clan classic says: ‘When the Year Star exits left there is a harvest year; when it exits right there is none.
translations.idiomatic: The Shi manual reads Jupiter’s left exit as plenty, right as famine.

sentences.zh: 』今金木俱東，歲星在南，是為出右，恐年穀不成，宋人饑也。

translations.literal: ’ Now metal and wood both lie east; the Year Star is on the south—that is exiting to the right—I fear the year’s grain will not ripen and the people of Song will hunger.
translations.idiomatic: Both planets east with Jupiter south signals “right exit”—famine may stalk Song.

sentences.zh: 陛下宜審詳明堂布政之務，然後妖異可消，五緯順序矣。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought carefully to examine the tasks of spreading policy in the Bright Hall—then prodigies can be dispelled and the five planet-cords will follow order.
translations.idiomatic: Audit Bright-Hall governance and planetary order will right itself.

sentences.zh: 四事：《易傳》曰：『陽無德則旱，陰僭陽亦旱。
