translations.literal: Fourth matter: the Yi commentary says: ‘When yang lacks virtue there is drought; when yin usurps yang there is also drought.
translations.idiomatic: Fourth: weak sovereign yang or dominant ministerial yin both parched the land.

sentences.zh: 』陽無德者，人君恩澤不施於人也。

translations.literal: ’ Yang lacking virtue means the lord of men’s grace does not reach the people.
translations.idiomatic: Weak yang means royal grace never reaches the fields.

sentences.zh: 陰僭陽者，祿去公室，臣下專權也。

translations.literal: Yin usurping yang means stipends leave the ducal house and ministers monopolize power.
translations.idiomatic: Usurping yin means revenue leaks from the throne to ministerial cliques.

sentences.zh: 自冬涉春，訖無嘉澤，數有西風，反逆時節。

translations.literal: From winter through spring to the end there has been no timely blessing rain; several times west winds have blown, contrary to the seasonal nodes.
translations.idiomatic: Winter into spring brought no healing rain—only ill-timed west winds.

sentences.zh: 朝廷勞心，廣為禱祈，薦祭山川，暴龍移市。

translations.literal: The court has wearied its heart, prayed broadly, sacrificed to mountains and rivers, exposed dragon idols and shifted the market.
translations.idiomatic: The court prayed everywhere—mountains, rivers, dragon rites, market shifts.

sentences.zh: 臣聞皇天感物，不為偽動，災變應人，要在責已。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that August Heaven responds to things and is not stirred by pretense; calamity and transformation answer to man—the essential lies in blaming oneself.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven ignores sham ritual—true reform begins with the throne’s self-blame.

sentences.zh: 若令雨可請降，水可攘止，則歲無隔並，太平可待。

translations.literal: If rain could be prayed down and flood could be brushed aside, then years would never fail and great peace could be counted on.
translations.idiomatic: If prayer alone fixed weather, drought would never plague us.

sentences.zh: 然而災害不息者，患不在此也。

translations.literal: Yet disasters do not cease—the trouble is not located here.
translations.idiomatic: Calamity persists because the fault lies elsewhere.

sentences.zh: 立春以來，未見朝廷賞錄有功，表顯有德，存問孤寡，賑恤貧弱，而但見洛陽都官奔車東西，收系纖介，牢獄充盈。

translations.literal: Since the Beginning of Spring I have not seen the court reward merit, manifest virtue, inquire after widows and orphans, or relieve the poor and weak—only Luoyang capital officials’ carts racing east and west, seizing trifling matters, prisons stuffed full.
translations.idiomatic: Since spring I see no rewards for virtue—only capital police hauling petty suspects until jails burst.

sentences.zh: 臣聞恭陵火處，比有光曜，明此天災，非人之咎。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that at the Gongling fire site there has lately been light flashing—this clarifies that this Heaven-sent calamity is not human guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Fresh light over Gongling proves Heaven’s fire, not arson.

sentences.zh: 丁丑大風，掩蔽天地。

translations.literal: On dingchou a great wind covered Heaven and Earth.
translations.idiomatic: On dingchou a gale blackened sky and land.

sentences.zh: 風者號令，天之威怒，皆所以感悟人君忠厚之戒。

translations.literal: Wind is command and instruction; it is Heaven’s awesome wrath—all serve to awaken the lord of men to warnings of loyalty and solidity.
translations.idiomatic: Wind carries Heaven’s command—warning the throne toward steadfast rule.

sentences.zh: 又連月無雨，將害宿麥。

translations.literal: Further, for months there has been no rain—it will harm the overwintering wheat.
translations.idiomatic: Month-long drought threatens the winter wheat.

sentences.zh: 若一穀不登，則饑者十三四矣。

translations.literal: If one staple fails, thirty or forty percent will go hungry.
translations.idiomatic: Lose one crop and a third of the people starve.

sentences.zh: 陛下誠宜廣被恩澤，貸贍元元。

translations.literal: Your Majesty truly ought broadly extend grace and lend relief to the common folk.
translations.idiomatic: Spread mercy and aid the commoners.

sentences.zh: 昔堯遭九年之水，人有十載之蓄者，簡稅防災，為其方也。

translations.literal: Formerly Yao suffered nine years of flood, yet people kept ten years’ surplus—light taxes and guarding against disaster were the means for that.
translations.idiomatic: Yao’s flood lasted nine years yet granaries stayed full—light taxes and planning.

sentences.zh: 願陛下早宣德澤，以應天功。

translations.literal: I hope Your Majesty will soon spread virtuous moisture to answer Heaven’s work.
translations.idiomatic: Issue generous relief now to match Heaven’s mandate.

sentences.zh: 若臣言不用，朝政不改者，立夏之後乃有澍雨，於今之際未可望也。

translations.literal: If your subject’s words are not adopted and court policy is unchanged, then only after the Beginning of Summer will there be timely rain—at present it cannot be hoped for.
translations.idiomatic: Ignore reform and rain waits till summer—hope nothing sooner.

sentences.zh: 若政變於朝而天不雨，則臣為誣上，愚不知量，分當鼎鑊。

translations.literal: If policy changes at court yet Heaven still withholds rain, then your subject has deceived his sovereign—I am foolish and ignorant of measure and deserve the cauldron.
translations.idiomatic: If reform fails to bring rain, boil me for lying.

sentences.zh: 書奏，特詔拜郎中，辭病不就，即去歸家。

translations.literal: When the memorial was presented, by special edict he was appointed Gentleman of the Palace; he pleaded illness and did not take office, then departed and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor named him gentleman of the palace; feigning illness he fled home.

sentences.zh: 至四月京師地震，遂陷。

translations.literal: In the fourth month the capital suffered an earthquake; ground subsided.
translations.idiomatic: Fourth month: the capital quaked and sank in places.

sentences.zh: 其夏大旱。

translations.literal: That summer there was great drought.
translations.idiomatic: Summer brought crushing drought.

sentences.zh: 秋，鮮卑入馬邑城，破代郡兵。

translations.literal: In autumn the Xianbei entered Mayi city and defeated the Dai commandery troops.
translations.idiomatic: Autumn: Xianbei took Mayi and routed Dai’s army.

sentences.zh: 明年，西羌寇隴右。

translations.literal: The next year the Western Qiang raided Longyou.
translations.idiomatic: Next year western Qiang struck Longyou.

sentences.zh: 皆略如顗言。

translations.literal: In rough outline all matched what Yi had said.
translations.idiomatic: Events unfolded much as Lang Yi predicted.

sentences.zh: 後復公車徵，不行。

translations.literal: Later he was summoned again by imperial carriage; he did not go.
translations.idiomatic: Later summons by state carriage went unanswered.

sentences.zh: 同縣孫禮者，積惡凶暴，好遊俠，與其同里人常慕顗名德，欲與親善。

translations.literal: Sun Li of the same county had piled up evil and was fierce and violent, fond of wandering knights; together with a fellow villager he always admired Yi’s reputation and virtue and wished to be intimate with him.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Li, local bully and knight-errant fan, courted Lang Yi’s fame with a neighbor.

sentences.zh: 顗不顧，以此結怨，遂為禮所殺。

translations.literal: Yi paid him no heed; thereby they formed a grudge, and he was thereupon killed by Li.
translations.idiomatic: Lang Yi snubbed him; Sun Li nursed a grudge and murdered him.

sentences.zh: 襄楷字公矩，平原隰陰人也。

translations.literal: Xiang Kai, courtesy name Gongju, was a native of Xiyin in Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Kai (courtesy Gongju) came from Xiyin in Pingyuan commandery.

sentences.zh: 好學博古，善天文陰陽之術。

translations.literal: He loved learning and ranged antiquity broadly; he was skilled in astronomy and yin–yang arts.
translations.idiomatic: He was bookish, steeped in ancient lore, and adept at celestial and yin-yang calculation.

sentences.zh: 桓帝時，宦官專朝，政刑暴濫，又比失皇子，災異尤數。

translations.literal: During Emperor Huan’s reign eunuchs monopolized court; government and punishments were cruel and excessive; moreover imperial sons were repeatedly lost—prodigies piled unusually thick.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Huan the castrates ran the court, justice turned savage, royal heirs kept dying, and omens multiplied.

sentences.zh: 延熹九年，楷自家詣闕上疏曰：

translations.literal: In the ninth year of Yanxi, Kai from his home went to the palace and presented a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Yanxi 9: Xiang Kai left home, presented himself at the gates, and offered this memorial:

sentences.zh: 臣聞皇天不言，以文象設教。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that August Heaven does not speak; it sets forth teaching through patterned images.
translations.idiomatic: High Heaven is silent—it teaches through celestial signs.

sentences.zh: 堯、舜雖聖，必歷象日月星辰，察五緯所在，故能享百年之壽，為萬世之法。

translations.literal: Though Yao and Shun were sages, they necessarily regulated observation of sun, moon, and stars and surveyed where the five cords lay—therefore they could enjoy a hundred years’ span and be models for ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: Even Yao and Shun tracked the luminaries and five planets—hence long life and an enduring pattern for posterity.

sentences.zh: 臣竊見去歲五月，熒惑入太微，犯帝座，出端門，不軌常道。

translations.literal: Your subject observed last May that the Sparkling Deluder entered the Supreme Turret, violated the Emperor’s Seat, exited the Portal of Truth, and departed from its usual course.
translations.idiomatic: Last May Mars entered the Supreme Turret, crossed the Emperor’s Seat, left the Vermilion Portal, straying from its track.

sentences.zh: 其閏月良辰，太白入房，犯心小星，震動中耀。

translations.literal: In the intercalary month on an auspicious day Great White entered Bond, violated the lesser stars about the Heart, and shook the central brilliance.
translations.idiomatic: That leap month Venus entered Bond, brushed the Heart’s minor stars, and rocked the central blaze.

sentences.zh: 中耀，天王也；

translations.literal: The central brilliance is the celestial king;
translations.idiomatic: That central blaze is the celestial sovereign;

sentences.zh: 傍小星者，天王子也。

translations.literal: the lesser stars beside him are the celestial king’s sons.
translations.idiomatic: the lesser sparks beside him stand for imperial sons.

sentences.zh: 夫太微天廷，五帝之坐，而金火罰星揚光其中，於占，天子凶；

translations.literal: The Supreme Turret is Heaven’s court, the seats of the Five Thearchs; metal and fire, the punishing stars, throw their light within it—by divination it means the Son of Heaven is in peril;
translations.idiomatic: The Supreme Turret is the five emperors’ court—Venus and Mars flare there, portending the ruler’s danger;

sentences.zh: 又俱入房、心，法無繼嗣。

translations.literal: further, both have entered Bond and the Heart—by pattern there will be no successors.
translations.idiomatic: both intruding Bond and Heart forecast no heir.

sentences.zh: 今年歲星久守太微，逆行西至掖門，還切執漢。

translations.literal: This year the Year Star long guarded the Supreme Turret, retrograded west to the Side Gate, then returned cutting close to the Han asterism.
translations.idiomatic: This year Jupiter lingered in the Supreme Turret, reversed west toward the Rear Gate, then swung back hugging the celestial Han.

sentences.zh: 歲為木精，好生惡殺，而淹留不去者，咎在仁德不修，誅罰太酷。

translations.literal: The year star is wood’s essence—it loves life and hates killing—yet its lingering stay means the fault lies in unrepaired benevolent virtue and punishments too cruel.
translations.idiomatic: Jupiter cherishes life—its refusal to move indicts neglected mercy and brutal law.

sentences.zh: 前七年十二月，熒惑與歲星俱入軒轅，逆行四十餘日，而鄧皇后誅。

translations.literal: Seven years before, in the twelfth month the Sparkling Deluder and Year Star both entered the Chariot, retrograded forty-odd days, and Empress Deng was executed.
translations.idiomatic: Seven years back, Mars and Jupiter entered the Chariot, reversed forty days—and Empress Deng fell.

sentences.zh: 其冬大寒，殺鳥獸，害魚鱉，城傍竹柏之葉有傷枯者。

translations.literal: That winter’s cold was extreme—it slew birds and beasts, harmed fish and turtles; leaves of bamboo and cypress beside the walls withered.
translations.idiomatic: That winter froze wildlife and killed riverside bamboo and cypress.

sentences.zh: 臣聞於師曰：『柏傷竹枯，不出三年，天子當之。

translations.literal: Your subject heard from his teacher: ‘When cypress is wounded and bamboo withered, within three years the Son of Heaven will answer for it.
translations.idiomatic: My teacher warned: withered bamboo and cypress within three years touches the ruler.

sentences.zh: 』今洛陽城中人夜無故叫呼，云有火光，人聲正喧，於占亦與竹柏枯同。

translations.literal: ’ Now the people of Luoyang by night for no reason cry out, saying there is firelight, human voices in uproar—by divination it matches the withering of bamboo and cypress.
translations.idiomatic: Luoyang now shrieks at night of ghost fires—same omen as the plants’ death.

sentences.zh: 自春夏以來，連有霜雹及大雨雷，而臣作威作福，刑罰急刻之所感也。

translations.literal: From spring and summer onward there have been repeated frost, hail, great rain, and thunder—this is what is stirred by those who arrogate power and favor and by punishments hasty and harsh.
translations.idiomatic: Since spring, frost, hail, and thunderclap storms follow eunuch arrogance and harsh justice.

sentences.zh: 太原太守劉質、南陽太守成晉，志除奸邪，其所誅剪，皆合人望，而陛下受閹豎之譖，乃遠加考逮。

translations.literal: Administrator Liu Zhi of Taiyuan and Administrator Cheng Jin of Nanyang aimed to purge wickedness—those they executed were all what popular expectation approved—yet Your Majesty accepted eunuchs’ slanders and imposed distant examination and arrest.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Zhi and Cheng Jin executed villains the people endorsed—you trusted castrates’ slander and had both men hauled away.

sentences.zh: 三公上書乞哀質等，不見采察，而嚴被譴讓。

translations.literal: The Three Excellencies memorialized begging pity for Zhi and others; they were not accepted for inspection but sternly rebuked.
translations.idiomatic: The three dukes pleaded for Liu Zhi—you ignored them and browbeat the ministers instead.

sentences.zh: 憂國之臣，將遂杜口矣。

translations.literal: Ministers who worry for the state will thereupon seal their mouths.
translations.idiomatic: Patriotic officials will soon fall silent.

sentences.zh: 臣聞殺無罪，誅賢者，禍及三世。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that killing the guiltless and executing the worthy brings disaster through three generations.
translations.idiomatic: Murdering innocents and killing worthies curses three generations.

sentences.zh: 自陛下即位以來，頻行誅伐，梁、寇、孫、鄧，並見族滅，其從坐者，又非其數。

translations.literal: Since Your Majesty mounted the throne executions have come in waves—Liang, Kou, Sun, Deng have all been extirpated as clans; those connected by implication are beyond reckoning.
translations.idiomatic: Your reign has massacred the Liang, Kou, Sun, and Deng lines—collateral victims number beyond count.

sentences.zh: 李雲上書，明主所不當諱，杜眾乞死，諒以感悟聖朝，曾無赦宥，而並被殘戮，天下之人，咸知其冤。

translations.literal: Li Yun memorialized on what an enlightened ruler ought not conceal; Du Zhong begged death in hope of awakening the sage court—yet neither received amnesty; both were butchered—the people of the realm all knew their injustice.
translations.idiomatic: Li Yun and Du Zhong died for frank counsel—no pardon—and everyone knew it was wrong.

sentences.zh: 漢興以來，未有拒諫誅賢，用刑太深如今者也。

translations.literal: Since Han arose never has there been rejection of remonstrance and execution of worthies with punishments as deep as today’s.
translations.idiomatic: Never in Han’s history has frank speech been punished this savagely.

sentences.zh: 永平舊典，諸當重論皆須冬獄，先請後刑，所以重人命也。

translations.literal: The Yongping old code required all cases meriting heavy sentence to await winter prison—first request, then execution—thereby valuing human life.
translations.idiomatic: The Yongping code kept capital cases for winter review—life mattered.

sentences.zh: 頃數十歲以來，州郡玩習，又欲避請讞之煩，輒托疾病，多死牢獄。

translations.literal: For several decades provinces and commanderies have grown accustomed and also wish to avoid the bother of requesting verdicts—they falsely plead illness; many die in jail.
translations.idiomatic: For decades magistrates dodge capital review by feigning sickness—prisoners rot awaiting trial.

sentences.zh: 長吏殺生自己，死者多非其罪，魂神冤結，無所歸訴，淫厲疾疫，自此而起。

translations.literal: Senior clerks decide life and death on their own—the dead are often not guilty of their crimes; souls knot in grievance with nowhere to appeal—pestilence and epidemic rise from this.
translations.idiomatic: Prefects play judge and executioner—innocent ghosts cry out and plague follows.

sentences.zh: 昔文王一妻，誕至十子，今宮女數千，未聞慶育。

translations.literal: Formerly King Wen had one wife and begat ten sons; today there are thousands of palace women—yet none has brought joyful births.
translations.idiomatic: King Wen’s one queen bore ten heirs—you house thousands yet sire none.

sentences.zh: 宜修德省刑，以廣《螽斯》之祚。

translations.literal: You ought to cultivate virtue and lighten punishments so as to widen the blessing of the ‘Locusts’ ode.
translations.idiomatic: Cultivate virtue, soften sentences, and earn the fertility hymned in ‘Locusts.’

sentences.zh: 又七年六月十三日，河內野王山上有龍死，長可數十丈。

translations.literal: Further, on the thirteenth of the sixth month of the seventh year a dragon died on the mountain at Yewang in Henei—its length was several dozen yards.
translations.idiomatic: Seventh year, sixth month, day thirteen: a dragon carcass dozens of yards long lay on Yewang mountain in Henei.

sentences.zh: 扶風有星隕為石，聲聞三郡。

translations.literal: In Fufeng a star fell and became stone—the sound was heard across three commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: Fufeng watched a meteorite strike—thunder heard three counties away.

sentences.zh: 夫龍形狀不一，小大無常，故《周易》況之大人，帝王以為符瑞。

translations.literal: Dragons take many shapes and no fixed size—therefore the Zhou Changes compares them to the great man; emperors take them as auspicious tokens.
translations.idiomatic: Dragons shift shape—the Changes likens them to sages; dynasties read them as omens.

sentences.zh: 或聞河內龍死，諱以為蛇。

translations.literal: Some hearing of the Henei dragon’s death euphemize it as a snake.
translations.idiomatic: Courtiers call the dead dragon a snake to hide the ill omen.

sentences.zh: 夫龍能變化，蛇亦有神，皆不當死。

translations.literal: Dragons can transform; snakes too have spirit—neither ought to die.
translations.idiomatic: Dragons and divine snakes should not simply die.

sentences.zh: 昔秦之將衰，華山神操璧以授鄭客，曰『今年祖龍死』，始皇逃之，死於沙丘。

translations.literal: Formerly when Qin was about to decline the god of Mount Hua handed a jade disk to a traveler from Zheng saying ‘This year the Ancestor Dragon dies’—the First Emperor fled the omen yet died at Shaqiu.
translations.idiomatic: Qin’s fall was foretold when Mount Hua’s spirit promised ‘the First Emperor dies this year.’

sentences.zh: 王莽天鳳二年，訛言黃山宮有死龍之異，後漢誅莽，光武復興。

translations.literal: In Wang Mang’s Tianfeng 2 false rumor spoke of dead dragons at Huangshan Palace—later Han executed Mang and Guangwu revived.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang’s reign heard rumors of dead dragons—Han restored thereafter.

sentences.zh: 虛言猶然，況於實邪？

translations.literal: Empty rumor still proved true—how much more solid fact?
translations.idiomatic: If rumor fulfilled itself, how much more must reality?

sentences.zh: 夫星辰麗天，猶萬國之附王者也。

translations.literal: The asterisms cling to heaven like the myriad states cleaving to a king.
translations.idiomatic: Stars ornament the sky as states cling to their ruler.

sentences.zh: 下將畔上，故星亦畔天。

translations.literal: When those below will rebel against superiors, then stars too rebel against heaven.
translations.idiomatic: When vassals mutiny, stars desert their stations.

sentences.zh: 石者安類，墜者失勢。

translations.literal: Stone is the class of stability; falling means loss of power.
translations.idiomatic: Stone stands for firmness—fallen stone means lost mandate.

sentences.zh: 春秋五石隕宋，其後襄公為楚所執。

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn records five stones falling in Song—afterward Duke Xiang was seized by Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Five meteors in Song preceded Duke Xiang’s capture by Chu.

sentences.zh: 秦之亡也，石隕東郡。

translations.literal: When Qin perished, stones fell in Dong commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Qin’s end saw meteorites in Dong commandery.

sentences.zh: 今損扶風，與先帝園陵相近，不有大喪，必有畔逆。

translations.literal: Now damage strikes Fufeng—near the late emperors’ mausolea—there will either be great mourning or great rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: Fufeng’s fall strikes near imperial tombs—expect death or revolt.

sentences.zh: 案春秋以來及古帝王，未有河清及學門自壞者也。

translations.literal: Searching from the Spring and Autumn down through ancient emperors, never has there been a Yellow River running clear nor a school gate collapsing of itself.
translations.idiomatic: History records no clear Yellow River nor self-ruined academy gate until now.

sentences.zh: 臣以為河者，諸侯位也。

translations.literal: Your subject believes the river stands for the feudal lords’ station.
translations.idiomatic: The river images regional lords.

sentences.zh: 清者屬陽，濁者屬陰。

translations.literal: Clear belongs to yang; turbid belongs to yin.
translations.idiomatic: Clear water is yang; muddy is yin.

sentences.zh: 河當濁而反清者，陰欲為陽，諸侯欲為帝也。

translations.literal: When the river ought to be turbid yet turns clear, yin wishes to become yang—the feudal lords wish to become emperor.
translations.idiomatic: A muddy river running clear means regional powers aim at the throne.

sentences.zh: 太學，天子教化之宮，其門無故自壞者，言文德將喪，教化廢也。

translations.literal: The Imperial Academy is the palace where the Son of Heaven transforms through teaching—when its gate for no reason collapses of itself it means patterned virtue will perish and instruction will be abandoned.
translations.idiomatic: The university gate’s ruin signals collapsing moral education.

sentences.zh: 京房《易傳》曰：『河水清，天下平。

translations.literal: Jing Fang’s Commentary on the Changes states: ‘When the Yellow River runs clear, the realm is at peace.
translations.idiomatic: Jing Fang promised peace when the river clears.

sentences.zh: 』今天垂盡，地吐妖，人厲疫，三者並時而有河清，猶春秋麟不當見而見，孔子書之以為異也。

translations.literal: ’ Now Heaven nears exhaustion, Earth vomits prodigies, men suffer pestilence—these three coincide yet the river runs clear—like the unicorn appearing in spring and autumn though it ought not—Confucius recorded it as strange.
translations.idiomatic: Yet heaven dims, earth disgorges monsters, plague spreads—still the river clears—unnatural as Confucius’ unicorn.

sentences.zh: 臣前上瑯邪宮崇受幹吉神書，不合明聽。

translations.literal: Your subject earlier memorialized about Gong Chong of Langye receiving Gan Ji’s divine book—it did not accord with enlightened hearing.
translations.idiomatic: I earlier cited Gong Chong’s scripture from Gan Ji—you dismissed it.

sentences.zh: 臣聞布穀鳴子孟夏，蟋蟀吟於始秋，物有微而誌信，人有賤而言忠。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard the cuckoo calls in early summer, crickets chirp at autumn’s start—creatures may be minute yet keep faith in season; men may be low yet speak loyalty.
translations.idiomatic: Small birds keep season; humble men may speak truth.

sentences.zh: 臣雖至賤，誠願賜清閑，極盡所言。

translations.literal: Though your subject is utterly base, he truly begs leisure to exhaust what he would say.
translations.idiomatic: Though lowest-born, I beg a hearing to finish my plea.

sentences.zh: 書奏不省。

translations.literal: When the writing was presented it was not examined.
translations.idiomatic: The throne filed my memorial unread.

sentences.zh: 十餘日，復上書曰：

translations.literal: After more than ten days he again presented a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Ten days later he submitted again:

sentences.zh: 臣伏見太白北入數日，復出東方，其占當有大兵，中國弱，四夷強。

translations.literal: Your subject has seen Great White sink northward several days then emerge east—by divination there ought to be great war—the Middle Kingdom weak, the four barbarians strong.
translations.idiomatic: Venus dipped north, rose east—great wars loom: China weak, frontier strong.

sentences.zh: 臣又推步，熒惑今當出而潛，必有陰謀。

translations.literal: Your subject has further calculated that the Sparkling Deluder ought to appear yet hides—there must be secret plotting.
translations.idiomatic: Mars should shine yet hides—conspiracy stirs.

sentences.zh: 皆由獄多冤結，忠臣被戮。

translations.literal: All because prisons knot with injustice and loyal ministers are slaughtered.
translations.idiomatic: Cause lies in unjust jails and murdered loyalists.

sentences.zh: 德星所以久守執法，亦為此也。

translations.literal: For this reason the virtue star long guards the Executioner constellation.
translations.idiomatic: Hence Jupiter lingers by the justice stars.

sentences.zh: 陛下宜承天意，理察冤獄，為劉質、成晉虧除罪辟，追錄李雲、杜眾等子孫。

translations.literal: Your Majesty ought to answer Heaven’s intent, investigate unjust prisons, clear Liu Zhi and Cheng Jin of crimes, and posthumously enroll the descendants of Li Yun and Du Zhong.
translations.idiomatic: Clear Liu Zhi and Cheng Jin, restore Li Yun’s and Du Zhong’s heirs.

sentences.zh: 夫天子事天不孝，則日食星鬥。

translations.literal: When the Son of Heaven serves Heaven unfilially, then there is solar eclipse and the stars contend.
translations.idiomatic: Impious service of Heaven brings eclipses and battling stars.

sentences.zh: 比年日食於正朔，三光不明，五緯錯戾。

translations.literal: In recent years there have been solar eclipses at new moon—the three luminaries lack clarity; the five cords run askew.
translations.idiomatic: Lately eclipses hit new moons—sun, moon, stars blur; planets stray.

sentences.zh: 前者宮崇所獻神書，專以奉天地順五行為本，亦有興國廣嗣之術。

translations.literal: The divine book Gong Chong presented earlier takes revering Heaven and Earth and obeying the five phases as root—it also contains arts for raising the state and widening heirs.
translations.idiomatic: Gong Chong’s scripture teaches cosmic harmony and restoring heirs.

sentences.zh: 其文易曉，參同經典，而順帝不行，故國胤不興，孝沖、孝質頻世短祚。

translations.literal: Its text is easy to understand and aligns with the classics—yet Emperor Shun did not enact it—therefore royal heirs did not flourish; Emperors Chong and Zhi reigned briefly generation after generation.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Shun ignored it—hence short-lived child emperors Chong and Zhi.

sentences.zh: 臣又聞之，得主所好，自非正道，神為生虐。

translations.literal: Your subject further hears that whatever the lord favors—if it is not the correct Way—the spirits visit cruelty.
translations.idiomatic: Illicit royal passions invite divine wrath.

sentences.zh: 故周衰，諸侯以力徵相尚，於是夏育、申休、宋萬、彭生、任鄙之徒生於其時。

translations.literal: Therefore when Zhou declined the feudal lords honored force in rivalry—thereupon heroes like Xia Yu, Shen Xiu, Song Wan, Peng Sheng, and Ren Bi arose in that age.
translations.idiomatic: Late Zhou’s obsession with brawn bred bully champions.

sentences.zh: 殷紂好色，妲已是出。

translations.literal: Yin Zhou loved beauty—thereupon Daji appeared.
translations.idiomatic: Shang’s lust produced Daji.

sentences.zh: 葉公好龍，真龍遊廷。

translations.literal: Lord Ye loved dragons—true dragons wandered his court.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Ye’s dragon hobby summoned real dragons.

sentences.zh: 今黃門常侍，天刑之人，陛下愛待，兼倍常寵，系嗣未兆，豈不為此？

translations.literal: Today the Yellow Gate attendants are men heaven-marked for punishment—Your Majesty loves and favors them several-fold beyond usual—is heirlessness solely without relation to this?
translations.idiomatic: You pamper heaven-cursed eunuchs—no coincidence you lack sons.

sentences.zh: 天官宦者星不在紫宮而在天市，明當給使主市裏也。

translations.literal: The celestial officials place the Eunuch Star not in the Purple Palace but in the Celestial Market—making clear they ought to serve market alleys.
translations.idiomatic: Astrology seats eunuch stars in the market—not the palace.

sentences.zh: 今乃反處常伯之位，實非天意。

translations.literal: Now they occupy Constant Attendant seats—truly not Heaven’s intent.
translations.idiomatic: Yet they hold ministerial rank—against Heaven’s design.

sentences.zh: 又聞宮中立黃、老、浮屠之祠。

translations.literal: Further your subject hears that within the palace shrines to Huangdi, Laozi, and the Buddha have been raised.
translations.idiomatic: Shrines to Huang-Lao and Buddha now fill the palace.

sentences.zh: 此道清虛，貴尚無為，好生惡殺，省欲去奢。

translations.literal: This Way is empty clarity—it esteems non-action, loves life and hates killing, trims desire and casts off extravagance.
translations.idiomatic: Those teachings prize emptiness, non-action, life, and austerity.

sentences.zh: 今陛下嗜欲不去，殺罰過理，既乖其道，豈獲其祚哉！

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty does not cast off craving; killing and fines exceed reason—you already oppose their Way—how could you obtain their blessing!
translations.idiomatic: You indulge appetites and slaughter—contradicting the very shrines you built.

sentences.zh: 或言老子入夷狄為浮屠。

translations.literal: Some say Laozi entered the barbarians and became the Buddha.
translations.idiomatic: Legend makes Laozi the Buddha among foreigners.

sentences.zh: 浮屠不三宿桑下，不欲久生恩愛，精之至也。

translations.literal: The Buddha does not lodge three nights beneath a mulberry—unwilling to prolong fond attachment—such is ultimate refinement.
translations.idiomatic: Buddha avoids mulberry shade overnight—no lingering attachment.

sentences.zh: 天神遺以好女，浮屠曰：『此但革囊盛血。

translations.literal: Heaven sent him a beautiful woman; the Buddha said: ‘This is only a leather sack holding blood.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven offered a beauty; Buddha called her a skin bag of blood.

sentences.zh: 』遂不眄之。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon he did not glance at her.
translations.idiomatic: He never looked again.

sentences.zh: 其守一如此，乃能成道。

translations.literal: His guarding unity was like this—only thus could he complete the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Such single-minded renunciation completes awakening.

sentences.zh: 今陛下淫女艷婦，極天下之麗，甘肥飲美，單天下之味，奈何欲如黃、老乎？

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty lusts after lovely women—summoning the realm’s fairest—savors rich drink and finest flavors—how could you hope to match Huangdi and Laozi?
translations.idiomatic: You hoard beauties and delicacies—hardly Huang-Lao detachment.

sentences.zh: 書上，即召詣尚書問狀。

translations.literal: When the memorial went up he was at once summoned to the Masters of Writing to answer for his charges.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial brought immediate summons before the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 楷曰：『臣聞古者本無宦臣，武帝末，春秋高，數遊後宮，始置之耳。

translations.literal: Kai said: ‘In antiquity there were originally no palace attendants; at the end of Emperor Wu’s reign, when he was advanced in years and often toured the inner palace, they first were installed.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Kai replied: antiquity had no eunuchs until aging Emperor Wu toured the harem.

sentences.zh: 後稍見任，至於順帝，遂益繁熾。

translations.literal: Afterward they were gradually trusted; down to Emperor Shun they grew ever more numerous and blazing.
translations.idiomatic: They grew through Shun’s reign into a blaze.

sentences.zh: 今陛下爵之，十倍於前。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty ennobles them tenfold compared with earlier.
translations.idiomatic: You heap titles on them tenfold beyond old practice.

sentences.zh: 至今無繼嗣者，豈獨好之而使之然乎？

translations.literal: That to the present there are no heirs—is it only because you favor them that it has become thus?
translations.idiomatic: Still no heir—is favoring them unrelated?

sentences.zh: 』尚書上其對，詔下有司處正。

translations.literal: ’ The Masters of Writing presented his reply; an edict ordered responsible offices to determine judgment.
translations.idiomatic: The secretariat relayed his answers; the throne ordered trial.

sentences.zh: 尚書承旨奏曰：『其宦者之官，非近世所置。

translations.literal: The Masters of Writing, accepting instruction, memorialized: ‘The offices of eunuchs are not an invention of recent ages.
translations.idiomatic: Officials answered: eunuch posts are ancient.

sentences.zh: 漢初張澤為大謁者，佐絳侯誅諸呂；

translations.literal: At the beginning of Han Zhang Ze was Chief Herald and aided the Marquis of Jiang in executing the Lü clan.
translations.idiomatic: Early Han’s Zhang Ze helped purge the Lüs.

sentences.zh: 孝文使趙談參乘，而子孫昌盛。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen had Zhao Tan ride in the secondary chariot—and his descendants flourished.
translations.idiomatic: Wen used Zhao Tan—his line thrived.

sentences.zh: 楷不正辭理，指陳要務，而析言破律，違背經藝，假借星宿，偽托神靈，造合私意，誣上罔事。

translations.literal: Kai did not straighten his rhetoric and principle or point out essential tasks—instead he split phrases to break statutes, violated the classics, borrowed constellations, falsely lodged spirits, concocted private meanings, slandering superiors and deceiving affairs.
translations.idiomatic: They charged Xiang Kai with twisting law and apocrypha to slander the throne.

sentences.zh: 請下司隸，正楷罪法，收送洛陽獄。

translations.literal: Request dispatch to the Metropolitan Superintendent to rectify Kai’s offense by law and collect and send him to Luoyang prison.
translations.idiomatic: They sought prison under the metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 』帝以楷言雖激切，然皆天文恒象之數，故不誅，猶司寇論刑。

translations.literal: ’ The emperor because Kai’s words, though sharp, all matched constant astronomical patterns did not execute him—still let the Minister of Justice debate punishment.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor spared him—his stars matched nature—but referred sentence to the judiciary.

sentences.zh: 初，順帝時，瑯邪宮崇詣闕，上其師幹吉於曲陽泉水上所得神書百七十卷，皆縹白素朱介青首朱目，號《太平清領書》。

translations.literal: Earlier, during Emperor Shun’s time, Gong Chong of Langye came to the palace and presented the divine book his teacher Gan Ji had obtained at the spring on Mount Quyang—one hundred seventy scrolls—all pale silk with vermilion borders, green headers, and vermilion titles, styled the Scripture of Great Peace with Pure Guidance.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Gong Chong offered Gan Ji’s 170-scroll Taiping scripture from the Quyang spring—lace-bound in pale silk.

sentences.zh: 其言以陰陽五行為家，而多巫覡雜語。

translations.literal: Its speech takes yin–yang and five phases as its basis and contains much shamanistic jargon.
translations.idiomatic: It mixes five-phase lore with shaman chatter.

sentences.zh: 有司奏崇所上妖妄不經，乃收臧之。

translations.literal: The responsible offices memorialized that what Chong presented was deceitful and uncanonical—it was therefore confiscated and stored.
translations.idiomatic: Courts ruled it heterodox and impounded it.

sentences.zh: 後張角頗有其書焉。

translations.literal: Later Zhang Jiao largely possessed its book.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Jiao later exploited that text.

sentences.zh: 論曰：古人有云：『善言天者，必有驗於人。

translations.literal: The judgment states: The ancients had a saying: ‘Those who speak well of Heaven must have verification in mankind.
translations.idiomatic: The historian notes: heavenly discourse needs human proof.

sentences.zh: 』而張衡亦云：『天文歷數，陰陽占候，今所宜急也。

translations.literal: ’ And Zhang Heng also said: ‘Astronomy, calendrics, yin–yang divination—these are what ought urgently to be cultivated today.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng urged mastery of astronomy and omen lore.

sentences.zh: 』郎顗、襄楷能仰瞻俯察，參諸人事，禍福吉凶既應，引之教義亦明。

translations.literal: ’ Lang Yi and Xiang Kai could look up to the sky and scan the earth, check their reading against human affairs—blessing and disaster, good and ill, matched events; the doctrines they invoked were likewise clear.
translations.idiomatic: Lang Yi and Xiang Kai read heaven against human affairs, and their forecasts matched events; the morals they drew from those signs were plain enough.

sentences.zh: 此蓋道術所以有補於時，後人所當取鑒者也。

translations.literal: This is how the techniques of the Way can aid the times and what later generations ought to take as a mirror.
translations.idiomatic: That is how omen lore can serve the age, and why posterity should study it as a warning.

sentences.zh: 然而其敝好巫，故君子不以專心焉。

translations.literal: Yet their defect is a fondness for shamanry—therefore the gentleman does not fix his mind solely on it.
translations.idiomatic: The trouble is their taste for occult shamanry, so a true gentleman does not treat such arts as the whole of wisdom.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：仲桓術深，蒲車屢尋。

translations.literal: The eulogy says: Zhonghuan’s arts were deep; the rush-wheeled summoning chariot was repeatedly sent to seek him.
translations.idiomatic: Hymn: Yang Hou’s learning ran deep; the court sent the rush-wheeled cart again and again to call him in.

sentences.zh: 蘇竟飛書，清我舊陰。

translations.literal: Su Jing’s flying letter cleared our old northern shadow.
translations.idiomatic: Su Jing’s missive from afar dispelled the shadow that lay on the north.

sentences.zh: 襄、郎災戒，實由政淫。

translations.literal: Xiang and Lang’s warnings of disaster—truly came from depraved government.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Kai and Lang Yi warned of calamity because policy had grown corrupt.
